Credentials dataclass: __repr__ masks client_secret as '***'; SDK never reads credentials from environment variables. httpx async client configured with verify=True (default CA bundle) and minimum TLS 1.2 via ssl_context; no ssl_context override exposed in public API to prevent accidental verify=False. Logging: structured log records at INFO for submission lifecycle events include envelope_id and court_system fields; document content bytes and Credentials fields are never passed to logger. SubmissionValidationError messages include field_violations list with field name and constraint description but never document content. Compliance with state e-filing rules: CourtEnvelopeValidator enforces per-court constraints sourced from Tyler Technologies certified integration requirements and Odyssey court standing orders; violations raised before any court submission attempt.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: PyPI distribution via hatch build producing sdist and wheel. GitHub Actions ci.yml: ruff lint, mypy --strict type check, pytest test matrix across Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 with coverage gate 90%, responses library for HTTP mocking of Tyler SOAP and Odyssey REST responses. GitHub Actions publish.yml triggered on tag v* using trusted publisher OIDC (no API key stored in repo secrets): hatch build, twine upload to PyPI. Pre-commit hooks: ruff format, mypy, pytest. CHANGELOG.md maintained with semver entries. Test PyPI publish on release candidate tags (v*-rc*) for pre-release validation. No runtime infrastructure to operate; SDK is a library.
target_output.observability: Python stdlib logging integration: consumers configure handlers; SDK emits structured records with fields: event_type (submission_initiated/submission_accepted/submission_rejected/poll_timeout), envelope_id, court_system, submission_id, duration_ms. Log level usage: DEBUG for HTTP request/response headers (body omitted), INFO for submission lifecycle state transitions, WARNING for retry attempts with attempt count and wait duration, ERROR for SubmissionValidationError and CourtSystemError with violation details. SDK provides a configure_default_logging(level=logging.INFO) convenience function for consumer setup.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost; PyPI hosting is free. SDK is async and stateless; consumers scale by instantiating concurrent EfilingClient instances or using asyncio.gather for parallel submissions. zeep WSDL caching eliminates repeated SOAP schema fetches; consumers should reuse EfilingClient instance across requests. Optional SQLite journal adds negligible disk overhead for submission metadata only.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (Weeks 1-6): pydantic FilingEnvelope and domain models, TylerFileServeAdapter SOAP implementation via zeep, OAuth token management with auto-refresh, CourtEnvelopeValidator with Tyler court_code allowlist and service_contacts validation (ISS-001 fix), EfilingClient facade, tenacity retry, SubmissionJournal optional SQLite, GitHub Actions CI + PyPI publish, 90% test coverage. Phase 2 (Weeks 7-10): OdysseyAdapter REST implementation, JurisdictionRegistry with Odyssey court rules, batch filing support with concurrent asyncio submission, resume_pending() journal method for interrupted batches. Phase 3 (Weeks 11-14): Mock court system adapter for consumer integration testing without live credentials, Catalis adapter stub, extended jurisdiction registry for top 20 state court systems by filing volume.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy --strict enforced in CI; all public API functions fully type-annotated. ruff for lint and format. pytest with pytest-asyncio for async tests; responses library for deterministic HTTP mock. Test structure mirrors src layout: tests/test_client.py, tests/adapters/test_tyler.py, tests/adapters/test_odyssey.py, tests/test_validators.py. Every public method has a docstring with parameter descriptions and raises documentation. Semantic versioning: breaking API changes increment major version; new features minor; patches for bug fixes. Breaking changes documented in CHANGELOG.md with migration guide.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Tyler Technologies WSDL schema changes break zeep serialization on new court system releases. Mitigation: pin Tyler WSDL URL to versioned endpoint; integration tests mock against a captured WSDL snapshot; release notes monitor Tyler API changelogs. Risk: CourtEnvelopeValidator jurisdiction rules file (jurisdictions.json) becomes stale as court standing orders change. Mitigation: jurisdiction rules versioned in SDK data directory with last_updated timestamps; consumers can override rules via JurisdictionRegistry.register() for jurisdictions not yet in the SDK. Tradeoff: synchronous-first API option not provided to keep codebase async-only; consumers needing sync interface can use asyncio.run() wrapper. Tradeoff: local SQLite journal uses stdlib sqlite3 without ORM to avoid adding SQLAlchemy as a dependency, accepting lower query flexibility.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: CourtEnvelopeValidator chosen as a separate class rather than pydantic model_validators because validation rules vary by court_system and must be loaded from the jurisdiction registry at validation time, not at model definition time. zeep chosen over manual XML string construction for Tyler SOAP because WSDL-driven serialization prevents XML namespace errors that trigger court rejection. async-first API with httpx chosen over requests because legal document automation vendors running batch filings benefit from concurrent asyncio submission without threading overhead.

metadata.notes: Round 2 court e-filing Python SDK; resolves ISS-001 via CourtEnvelopeValidator with JurisdictionRegistry for court-specific pre-submission validation; strong quality with detailed adapter architecture, pydantic models, and security controls.

profile.domain: Legal Technology - Document Review and Redaction

project_goal.value: Desktop application for attorneys to batch-redact PII from large PDF and TIFF document sets using configurable pattern rules and ML-assisted entity detection, optimized for high-throughput processing on local workstations handling millions of pages in litigation support contexts
project_goal.rationale: Litigation support teams spend 60-70% of review budget on manual redaction; ML-assisted detection with attorney confirmation reduces time per page by estimated 80%

target_users.value: Litigation support attorneys and paralegals at law firms and e-discovery vendors processing large document productions for PII redaction under FRCP Rule 26 and court protective orders
target_users.rationale: Primary users are legal review professionals needing high-throughput batch redaction with audit trail

project_class.rationale: Local workstation application required for processing of confidential litigation documents that must not leave the network perimeter

capabilities.rationale: Electron UI, Python sidecar for ML NER pipeline, SQLite local data, local spaCy NER model for PII detection

complexity_level.rationale: ML NER pipeline with multi-page PDF rendering, annotation state management, pixel-burn redaction, and high-throughput batch processing add significant complexity

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect redaction or redaction bypass on documents produced in litigation could expose privileged information and violate court protective orders

data_sensitivity.rationale: Litigation documents contain attorney-client privileged content, work product, and party PII under court protective order

external_exposure.rationale: All processing on local workstation; no network calls except optional license key validation; document content never leaves the machine

access_model.value: Local license key activation; single-user desktop session; no multi-user accounts; reviewer identity captured in review_sessions metadata for privilege log
access_model.rationale: Desktop app for individual reviewer workstations; reviewer identity for privilege log is entered at session start

feature_scope.value: Document set import (PDF and TIFF), ML NER pipeline for PII detection (names, SSNs, dates, addresses, phone numbers), suggested redaction annotation overlay in viewer, attorney confirm/reject/add manual redactions, configurable pattern rules (regex supplementing NER), batch processing mode across document set, pixel-burn PDF export with redaction applied to pixel layer, privilege log CSV generation, review session management with progress tracking
feature_scope.rationale: Complete batch redaction workflow from import through export

mvp_scope.value: PDF document set import, spaCy NER PII detection, annotation viewer with confirm/reject suggestions, manual redaction annotation, pixel-burn PDF export, basic privilege log CSV
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP covers the core redaction review workflow for PDF documents

security_baseline.value: No network calls for document content; TLS on license key validation only; SQLite database not shared across users; redaction annotations stored separately from document bytes; no cloud ML API calls; all NER runs locally
security_baseline.rationale: Court protective orders and privilege considerations prohibit document content from leaving the workstation

frontend_stack.value: Electron 31 with React 18, TypeScript, PDF.js for document rendering, react-konva for annotation overlay canvas
frontend_stack.rationale: Electron for cross-platform desktop; PDF.js for client-side rendering; react-konva for pixel-precise redaction box drawing

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 FastAPI local sidecar process; spaCy 3.7 with en_core_web_trf transformer model for NER; pdfplumber for text extraction; pikepdf for PDF manipulation and pixel-burn export
backend_stack.rationale: spaCy transformer model for high-accuracy NER; pdfplumber for text and coordinate extraction; pikepdf for non-destructive annotation and final pixel-burn

data_platform.value: SQLite for local review session state, document registry, redaction annotations, and entity detections; no cloud database
data_platform.rationale: All state local to workstation; SQLite zero-config embedded database for session persistence

hosting_target.value: Electron desktop distribution via installer (Windows NSIS, macOS DMG); auto-update via electron-updater from private S3 bucket
hosting_target.rationale: Desktop distribution with silent auto-update for firms deploying to multiple reviewer workstations

privacy_retention_policy.value: Review session data and annotations stored locally on reviewer workstation; document files remain in firm-controlled document set directory; no cloud sync; local data cleared on session archive per firm document retention policy
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Litigation hold and work product privilege require data to remain under firm control

future_scope.value: TIFF document support via OCR pipeline, custom fine-tuned NER model for domain-specific entity types, integration with e-discovery platforms (Relativity, Nuix) via watch folder
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap expanding beyond PDF MVP

constraints.value: Windows 10+ and macOS 12+ support; process 1 million pages under 24 hours on a workstation with 16 cores and 64GB RAM; no cloud API calls for NER; local spaCy model bundle under 500MB; installer under 800MB
constraints.rationale: Performance and air-gap constraints for litigation support workstations

observability_baseline.value: Sentry local crash reporting via Electron sentry SDK (no document content in crash reports); structured local log file at %APPDATA%/redact-tool/logs/ with rotation; review session progress metrics in SQLite for throughput dashboard
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash telemetry needed for support; local logs for troubleshooting; no external APM due to data sensitivity

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; GitHub Actions CI; desktop beta builds distributed to 3 pilot law firms for feedback
execution_preference.rationale: Pilot feedback loop with attorney users critical for annotation UX validation

compliance_context.value: FRCP Rule 26 proportionality in discovery; attorney-client privilege and work product doctrine; court protective order data handling requirements; privilege log accuracy obligations under FRCP Rule 26(b)(5)
compliance_context.rationale: Redaction errors or privilege log inaccuracies expose the producing party to sanctions and privilege waiver arguments

llm_integration.value: spaCy 3.7 en_core_web_trf transformer-based NER model for PII entity detection (PERSON, ORG, DATE, GPE, CARDINAL for SSN patterns); model bundled locally in app package; no external LLM API calls; optional custom model fine-tuning via spaCy CLI on domain-specific annotated data
llm_integration.rationale: All ML inference must run locally; transformer NER provides higher accuracy than rule-based patterns alone for legal document PII detection

project.goal: ML-assisted batch PII redaction desktop app for litigation support workstations
project.target_users: Litigation support attorneys and paralegals
project.timeline: PDF MVP 10 weeks, TIFF and integrations Phase 2

frontend.annotations: react-konva for redaction box overlay canvas

backend.pdf: pdfplumber for text extraction, pikepdf for manipulation
backend.ipc: Electron IPC main process to sidecar via HTTP localhost

data.db: SQLite via Python sqlite3 on sidecar side

devops.ci: GitHub Actions

reasoner_reviews.product: The annotation confirmation UX is the most critical design decision: attorneys must be able to rapidly confirm or reject NER suggestions (keyboard shortcuts for confirm/reject/next-page are essential) and add manual redaction boxes by drawing on the react-konva canvas. The privilege log CSV is a legal deliverable under FRCP Rule 26(b)(5) and must capture document_id, bates_number, redaction_type, redacted_entity_category, reviewer_id, and review_date for every applied redaction.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The Electron main process should spawn the Python FastAPI sidecar as a child process on app startup and communicate over localhost HTTP. This avoids the complexity of Electron IPC serialization for large document payloads. pdfplumber extracts text with bounding box coordinates per word; these coordinates map NER entity spans to page pixel positions for annotation overlay. For batch processing, the sidecar should expose a batch_process endpoint that queues documents and streams progress events back via SSE to avoid blocking the React UI.
reasoner_reviews.security: The most important security constraint is that the sidecar must reject any outbound HTTP request that is not to localhost or the license key validation endpoint. spaCy NER runs fully in-process on the sidecar; no model API calls made. Crash reports via Sentry must scrub any file path information that could reveal document names or review metadata. The SQLite database file should be in the app data directory, not in the document set directory, to prevent accidental inclusion in document productions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 1M pages per 24 hours on a 16-core workstation means approximately 11 pages per second or 86ms per page. spaCy en_core_web_trf transformer model processes approximately 50-100 pages per second with GPU acceleration or 5-15 pages per second on CPU-only. Batch mode must use Python multiprocessing to distribute page processing across available cores. pikepdf redaction burn is fast but serial; it can be parallelized per document rather than per page.

backend.service_design: Python 3.12 FastAPI sidecar with routers: /documents (import, list, page extraction), /ner (run entity detection on document or page range), /annotations (CRUD for redaction annotations), /export (trigger pixel-burn PDF export), /batch (batch processing job management with SSE progress stream), /session (review session management).
backend.api_patterns: FastAPI REST over localhost:8732. POST /documents/import accepts document set directory path, registers documents in SQLite. POST /ner/run/{document_id} triggers spaCy NER pipeline on extracted text, stores entity_detections. GET /batch/progress SSE stream emitting JSON progress events per processed document. POST /export/{document_id} burns confirmed redaction annotations into PDF pixel layer via pikepdf, returns output file path.
backend.background_jobs: Python multiprocessing Pool for batch NER processing across document set. Worker processes share the spaCy model loaded once per process. pikepdf export jobs run in a ThreadPoolExecutor (GIL-free C extension). SSE endpoint streams per-document progress events to Electron UI.
backend.failure_handling: SQLite review session state allows resume after workstation crash. If NER fails on a corrupt PDF, document is marked entity_detection_status=failed and batch continues. Export failures logged with pikepdf error and document marked export_status=failed for manual retry.

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process (src/main/): app lifecycle, sidecar spawn, auto-updater, menu. Renderer process (src/renderer/): React 18 app with React Router. Components: DocumentSetImport, ReviewDashboard, DocumentViewer (PDF.js + react-konva overlay), RedactionAnnotationPanel, BatchProgressMonitor, PrivilegeLogExport.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home: open or create review session. Document Set Import: select directory, preview document count, start import. Review Dashboard: document list with NER detection status, progress bars for batch mode. Document Viewer: page render via PDF.js, react-konva canvas overlay with color-coded suggestion boxes (yellow for NER suggestions, red for confirmed, blue for manual). Controls: confirm-all-on-page, reject-all-on-page, draw manual redaction box, keyboard J/K for next/previous suggestion. Batch Export: confirm all reviewed documents, trigger export, progress modal, output directory selection.
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: sessionStore (current review session, reviewer_id, progress stats), documentStore (document list, detection statuses), annotationStore (current document annotations, undo stack for annotation changes), batchStore (batch job status, progress events from SSE).
frontend.performance_notes: PDF.js renders each page as canvas; only the visible page and one prefetch page loaded in memory. react-konva annotation layer is a separate canvas composited over PDF.js canvas. Annotation store limited to current document to avoid memory pressure for document sets with 10K+ pages.

security.auth_design: Local license key activation via POST to license.redact-tool.com/validate with hardware fingerprint and key hash; no document content in license request. Session reviewer identity entered at session start as plain text name captured in review_sessions.reviewer_name.
security.privacy_controls: Sentry crash reports: document file paths replaced with [REDACTED_PATH] before submission; stack traces reviewed to ensure no document content in exception messages. SQLite database stored in %APPDATA%/redact-tool/sessions.db (Windows) or ~/Library/Application Support/redact-tool/sessions.db (macOS), never in document set directory.
security.security_risks: Risk of incomplete pixel-burn leaving redaction boxes as transparent overlays on top of visible text. Mitigation: pikepdf renders redaction boxes as filled black rectangles drawn directly on page content stream, not as PDF annotation overlays; export validation step confirms no underlying text extractable from exported PDF region.

data.entities: review_sessions (id INTEGER PK, session_name TEXT, reviewer_name TEXT, document_set_path TEXT, status TEXT, created_at TEXT, updated_at TEXT). document_sets (id INTEGER PK, session_id FK, file_path TEXT, file_name TEXT, page_count INTEGER, import_status TEXT, entity_detection_status TEXT, export_status TEXT). redaction_annotations (id INTEGER PK, document_id FK, page_number INTEGER, x REAL, y REAL, width REAL, height REAL, annotation_type TEXT CHECK(nlp_suggestion/manual), entity_category TEXT, status TEXT CHECK(suggested/confirmed/rejected), reviewer_confirmed_at TEXT). entity_detections (id INTEGER PK, document_id FK, page_number INTEGER, entity_type TEXT, entity_text_hash TEXT, start_char INTEGER, end_char INTEGER, confidence REAL, bbox_x REAL, bbox_y REAL, bbox_w REAL, bbox_h REAL, created_at TEXT). export_packages (id INTEGER PK, session_id FK, export_path TEXT, document_count INTEGER, redaction_count INTEGER, exported_at TEXT).
data.schema_notes: entity_detections stores entity_text_hash (SHA-256 of entity text) not plaintext entity content to avoid storing PII in the detection log. redaction_annotations bbox coordinates in PDF user space units for pikepdf compatibility.
data.migration_notes: SQLite schema versioned via user_version PRAGMA; migration scripts applied on app startup before sidecar binds port.

devops.deployment_topology: Electron desktop app with bundled Python sidecar. Python runtime and spaCy model bundled using PyInstaller one-dir mode into app.asar.unpacked/sidecar/. Electron builder packages Windows NSIS installer and macOS DMG. Auto-update: electron-updater checks private S3 bucket for latest.yml release manifest on startup.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint + tsc, Vitest for React component tests, pytest for Python sidecar with coverage gate 85%, electron-builder Windows and macOS cross-platform builds, S3 upload of installers and latest.yml for auto-update channel. Code signing: Windows authenticode via GitHub Actions secret certificate, macOS notarization via Apple Developer ID.
devops.cost_controls: No cloud runtime costs; S3 for installer distribution at minimal storage cost. spaCy transformer model bundled in installer eliminates per-inference API costs.

target_output.thinking_summary: Desktop app with a Python ML sidecar running locally. Key decisions: Electron spawns the FastAPI sidecar on startup communicating over localhost HTTP to avoid IPC serialization complexity for large PDF payloads; spaCy transformer NER runs fully local; pikepdf burns redactions as filled rectangles on PDF content stream (not overlay annotations) to prevent redaction bypass; SQLite session state enables crash recovery and privilege log generation. The LLM integration field triggers because ai_llm capability requires llm_integration contract field, which is satisfied by the local spaCy NER configuration.
target_output.title: Legal Document Batch Redaction Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput desktop application for litigation support attorneys to batch-redact PII from large PDF document productions using a local spaCy 3.7 transformer NER pipeline. Built with Electron 31 and React 18 for the review UI and a Python 3.12 FastAPI sidecar for document processing and ML inference. Attorneys review NER-suggested redaction annotations in a PDF.js viewer with react-konva overlay, confirming or rejecting per entity before triggering pikepdf pixel-burn export. Review sessions, annotations, and entity detections persist locally in SQLite for crash recovery and FRCP Rule 26(b)(5) privilege log generation. All document content and ML inference remain on the local workstation with no cloud API calls.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-process desktop architecture: Electron main process manages app lifecycle, spawns the Python FastAPI sidecar as a child process on startup communicating over localhost:8732, and handles auto-updates via electron-updater. The React renderer process communicates with the sidecar via fetch over localhost HTTP. The Python sidecar runs all document processing: pdfplumber text extraction with bounding box coordinates, spaCy en_core_web_trf NER for PII entity detection, SQLite persistence for session state and annotations, and pikepdf pixel-burn redaction export. Batch processing uses Python multiprocessing Pool distributing NER jobs across available CPU cores. The sidecar exposes a Server-Sent Events endpoint for real-time batch progress streaming to the Electron renderer. No network calls leave the workstation except the license key validation HTTPS request and auto-update manifest check from S3.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript 5, Vite (renderer build), PDF.js 4 (document rendering), react-konva 18 (annotation canvas), Zustand 4 (state management), TailwindCSS 3. Backend sidecar: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.111, uvicorn, spaCy 3.7 with en_core_web_trf transformer model, pdfplumber 0.11 (text and bbox extraction), pikepdf 9 (PDF manipulation and pixel-burn), sqlite3 stdlib (session and annotation persistence), PyInstaller (sidecar bundling). Build and distribution: electron-builder 25, NSIS (Windows), DMG (macOS), electron-updater, PyInstaller one-dir mode. CI: GitHub Actions, Vitest, pytest. Crash reporting: Sentry Electron SDK with path scrubbing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Document Set Import: attorney selects directory of PDF files; sidecar registers each document in SQLite, extracts page count via pdfplumber, returns import summary. NER Pipeline Execution: per document or batch, sidecar runs pdfplumber text extraction with word-level bounding boxes, passes text spans to spaCy NER, maps PERSON/ORG/DATE/GPE/CARDINAL entity spans to page bbox coordinates, stores entity_detections and creates suggested redaction_annotations with status=suggested. Annotation Review Viewer: PDF.js renders current page; react-konva overlays color-coded annotation boxes (yellow=suggested, green=confirmed, red=rejected, blue=manual); attorney uses keyboard shortcuts (C=confirm, R=reject, N=next suggestion, M=enter draw mode for manual box); confirm-all and reject-all page controls for bulk review. Batch Processing: queue all documents in document set for NER processing with SSE progress stream to BatchProgressMonitor; resume-capable via SQLite entity_detection_status field. Pixel-Burn Export: pikepdf draws confirmed annotations as filled black rectangles on PDF content stream per page, writes output PDF to export directory; export_packages record created for privilege log. Privilege Log Export: generates CSV with columns document_id, file_name, bates_number, page_number, redaction_category, redacted_entity_hash, reviewer_name, review_date per confirmed redaction_annotation.
target_output.system_components: Electron main process (src/main/index.ts): BrowserWindow lifecycle, sidecar child_process.spawn on app ready, sidecar health check with 30s timeout, IPC handlers for file dialog and auto-update. Python FastAPI sidecar (sidecar/): main.py with routers: /documents (import, list, page-text), /ner (run detection on document or page range), /annotations (CRUD), /batch (queue, status, /progress SSE stream), /export (pixel-burn trigger), /session (CRUD). spaCy NLP module (sidecar/nlp.py): loads en_core_web_trf on startup, expose process_page(text, bboxes) returning entity spans. SQLite schema managed by sidecar/db.py with user_version migration. PyInstaller spec bundles sidecar/ and spaCy model data into app.asar.unpacked/sidecar/. electron-updater configured to check S3 latest.yml on app focus.
target_output.workflows: Document Import and NER Batch Flow: Attorney opens app, creates review session with session_name and reviewer_name. Selects document set directory via Electron dialog. Renderer calls POST /documents/import; sidecar iterates PDF files, registers in document_sets table, returns count. Attorney clicks 'Run NER on All Documents'. Renderer calls POST /batch/start; sidecar creates batch job, spawns multiprocessing Pool with one worker per CPU core minus one. Each worker calls pdfplumber to extract page text and word bboxes, passes to spaCy NLP module, maps entity spans to page bbox coordinates, inserts entity_detections rows, creates redaction_annotations with status=suggested. Worker emits progress events consumed by SSE /batch/progress endpoint. Renderer BatchProgressMonitor updates document list status via SSE stream. Annotation Review and Confirmation Flow: Attorney opens document in DocumentViewer. Renderer fetches GET /annotations/{document_id} for current annotations. PDF.js renders first page; react-konva overlays annotation boxes. Attorney presses C to confirm highlighted suggestion (annotation status updated via PATCH /annotations/{id} to confirmed), R to reject (status=rejected), or draws manual box (POST /annotations with type=manual). Attorney navigates pages with arrow keys; annotationStore preloads next page annotations. On session complete, attorney clicks Export Reviewed Documents. Renderer calls POST /export/{document_id} for each reviewed document; sidecar pikepdf opens original PDF, iterates confirmed redaction_annotations per page, draws filled black rectangle at bbox coordinates on page content stream, writes output PDF to session export directory. Export Validation: after pikepdf export, sidecar calls pdfplumber on exported PDF to verify no text extractable from redacted regions; marks export_status=validated or export_status=validation_failed.
target_output.data_model: review_sessions (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, session_name TEXT NOT NULL, reviewer_name TEXT NOT NULL, document_set_path TEXT NOT NULL, status TEXT CHECK(active/archived/exported) DEFAULT active, total_documents INTEGER, processed_documents INTEGER, exported_documents INTEGER, created_at TEXT NOT NULL, updated_at TEXT NOT NULL). document_sets (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, session_id INTEGER FK review_sessions, file_path TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE, file_name TEXT NOT NULL, page_count INTEGER, import_status TEXT CHECK(pending/imported/failed), entity_detection_status TEXT CHECK(not_started/queued/processing/completed/failed), export_status TEXT CHECK(not_exported/exporting/exported/validation_failed), updated_at TEXT NOT NULL). redaction_annotations (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, document_id INTEGER FK document_sets, page_number INTEGER NOT NULL, x REAL NOT NULL, y REAL NOT NULL, width REAL NOT NULL, height REAL NOT NULL, annotation_type TEXT CHECK(nlp_suggestion/manual) NOT NULL, entity_category TEXT, confidence REAL, status TEXT CHECK(suggested/confirmed/rejected) NOT NULL DEFAULT suggested, reviewer_confirmed_at TEXT, created_at TEXT NOT NULL). entity_detections (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, document_id INTEGER FK document_sets, page_number INTEGER NOT NULL, entity_type TEXT NOT NULL, entity_text_hash TEXT NOT NULL, start_char INTEGER, end_char INTEGER, confidence REAL, bbox_x REAL, bbox_y REAL, bbox_w REAL, bbox_h REAL, created_at TEXT NOT NULL). export_packages (id INTEGER PK AUTOINCREMENT, session_id INTEGER FK review_sessions, export_directory TEXT NOT NULL, document_count INTEGER, total_redactions INTEGER, exported_at TEXT NOT NULL). Indexes: redaction_annotations(document_id, page_number), entity_detections(document_id, page_number).
target_output.api_design: FastAPI sidecar REST on localhost:8732. POST /sessions (create review session). GET /sessions/{id} (session detail with document counts). POST /documents/import {session_id, directory_path} (import document set, returns {document_count, total_pages}). GET /documents?session_id={id} (document list with detection and export status). POST /ner/run/{document_id} (trigger NER on single document, async). POST /batch/start {session_id} (start batch NER, returns job_id). GET /batch/{job_id}/progress (SSE stream: event data JSON {document_id, file_name, page_count, status, progress_pct}). GET /annotations/{document_id}?page={n} (annotation list for document page). POST /annotations (create manual annotation). PATCH /annotations/{id} {status} (confirm or reject). POST /export/{document_id} {output_dir} (trigger pikepdf pixel-burn, returns {output_path, validated: bool}). GET /privilege-log/{session_id} (returns CSV bytes of FRCP 26(b)(5) privilege log for session).
target_output.security_and_compliance: No network egress for document content: FastAPI sidecar binds to 127.0.0.1:8732 only; CORS restricted to Electron origin. spaCy NER runs in-process on sidecar: no HTTP calls to ML APIs. License key validation: HTTPS POST to license endpoint with hardware fingerprint and key hash only; no document metadata in request. Sentry crash reporting: Electron preload script wraps Sentry SDK to replace file paths matching document_set_path regex with [REDACTED_PATH] before event send; stack traces reviewed in Sentry to confirm no document content. SQLite database in app data directory not document directory: prevents accidental production of session.db in document exports. Redaction pixel-burn security: pikepdf draws filled black rectangles on PDF content stream (not PDF annotation objects); post-export pdfplumber verification confirms text not extractable from redacted regions. FRCP Rule 26(b)(5) privilege log: entity_text_hash (SHA-256) stored instead of plaintext entity to prevent the privilege log itself from being a discovery liability.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: electron-builder configuration: appId com.redact-tool.desktop, productName Redact Tool. Windows target: NSIS installer with custom install directory, registry entry for auto-update check, code-signed with Authenticode certificate via GitHub Actions secret. macOS target: DMG with notarization via Apple Developer ID and stapling via notarytool in GitHub Actions. PyInstaller spec (sidecar.spec): one-dir mode bundling Python 3.12 runtime, spaCy en_core_web_trf model data (bundled from spaCy model package), FastAPI and uvicorn, pdfplumber, pikepdf. Bundled into app.asar.unpacked/sidecar/. Electron main spawns sidecar executable with port 8732, monitors child process exit, logs to %APPDATA%/redact-tool/sidecar.log. Auto-update: electron-updater checks S3 bucket redact-tool-releases/latest.yml on app window focus; downloads delta update in background; prompts user to restart. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint + tsc, Vitest (renderer component tests), pytest sidecar tests with coverage gate 85%, electron-builder cross-compile (Windows on windows-latest runner, macOS on macos-latest), upload to S3 release bucket on tag push.
target_output.observability: Sentry Electron SDK configured with dsn and with beforeSend hook scrubbing file paths. Local structured log file at %APPDATA%/redact-tool/logs/app.log (Windows) or ~/Library/Logs/redact-tool/app.log (macOS) with daily rotation using Electron log library. Log fields: timestamp, level, component (main/renderer/sidecar), event_type, session_id, document_id (UUID reference not file path). Review session progress stored in review_sessions.processed_documents and review_sessions.exported_documents columns for in-app throughput dashboard. NER processing throughput metric: pages_per_second computed from entity_detections created_at delta displayed in BatchProgressMonitor.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No cloud runtime costs after initial distribution. PyInstaller bundled spaCy transformer model eliminates per-inference API fees. S3 storage for installer distribution approximately 2 GB per release at negligible cost. Performance: spaCy en_core_web_trf on 16-core workstation with Python multiprocessing Pool using 15 workers achieves approximately 10-15 pages per second CPU-only, meeting 1M pages per 24-hour SLA. GPU-enabled workstations with CUDA achieve 50+ pages per second. pikepdf pixel-burn parallelized per document across ThreadPoolExecutor workers.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (Weeks 1-10): Electron app shell with React 18, Python sidecar FastAPI skeleton, PDF.js document rendering, pdfplumber text extraction, spaCy NER pipeline, entity_detections storage, redaction_annotations CRUD, react-konva annotation overlay viewer with confirm/reject/manual controls, pikepdf pixel-burn export, privilege log CSV, SQLite schema with migration versioning, GitHub Actions CI, electron-builder Windows and macOS installers, Sentry crash reporting, beta distribution to 3 pilot firms. Phase 2 (Weeks 11-18): TIFF OCR pipeline via Tesseract, configurable regex pattern rules supplementing NER, batch SSE progress streaming, Relativity watch folder integration, custom spaCy fine-tuning CLI for domain entity types, export validation pdfplumber check. Phase 3 (Weeks 19-24): Nuix integration, privilege log FRCP 26(b)(5) extended fields, throughput analytics dashboard, Electron auto-update silent install mode for firm IT deployment.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Electron renderer. ESLint with react-hooks and import/no-cycle. Vitest for React component tests covering annotation confirm/reject state transitions and privilege log CSV generation. pytest for Python sidecar with coverage gate 85%: tests for NER bbox mapping, pikepdf pixel-burn and post-export validation, privilege log generation. Entity text never stored in plaintext in SQLite or log output; use entity_text_hash SHA-256 throughout. redaction_annotations bbox coordinates always in PDF user space units (points), not screen pixels, to ensure pikepdf and PDF.js coordinate systems are aligned. ADR documents for Electron-sidecar localhost HTTP choice over IPC, pikepdf content-stream redaction over annotation overlay approach, and entity_text_hash privacy design.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: pikepdf pixel-burn redaction bypass if attorney confirms annotation but export job silently fails; post-export pdfplumber verification is the mitigation but adds 10-20% to export time. Risk: spaCy en_core_web_trf transformer model NER misses entity occurrences on pages with unusual fonts or scanned image-based PDFs (no OCR in Phase 1); mitigation is attorney review step being mandatory and batch-reject-all being disabled by default. Tradeoff: PyInstaller sidecar bundling increases installer size to approximately 600MB (within 800MB constraint) but eliminates Python environment dependency issues on attorney workstations. Risk: SQLite database file corruption on hard workstation crash during batch write; mitigation is WAL journal mode enabling atomic commits and crash recovery. Tradeoff: localhost HTTP to sidecar over Electron IPC adds minimal latency (~0.5ms per call) but avoids serialization complexity for binary PDF page data passed as base64 or file path references.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Localhost HTTP chosen over Electron contextBridge IPC for sidecar communication because PDF page content passed as file path references avoids large byte array serialization across IPC boundary. pikepdf content-stream redaction chosen over PDF annotation redact objects because standard PDF annotation-based redaction requires a PDF reader to apply the redaction before printing; pixel-burn on content stream ensures redaction survives any PDF reader. entity_text_hash stored instead of plaintext entity text in SQLite to prevent the session database from becoming a discovery liability containing the same PII it was created to redact. spaCy en_core_web_trf transformer model chosen over en_core_web_sm for higher NER accuracy on legal document names and entity types, accepting larger model size within the 500MB constraint.

metadata.notes: First pass desktop app for batch PDF redaction; local spaCy NER sidecar; Electron with react-konva annotation overlay; pikepdf pixel-burn export; moderate quality with strong security and NER workflow sections; deployment section slightly thin on auto-update configuration details.

profile.domain: Legal Operations Platform Inter-Service SDK

project_goal.value: Go SDK providing typed client stubs auto-generated from Protobuf service definitions for contract management, billing, and matter tracking microservices within a distributed legal operations platform, with built-in service discovery, circuit breaking, and distributed tracing
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates hand-rolled HTTP clients in each consuming service and standardises cross-service reliability patterns across the platform

target_users.value: Internal platform engineers at the legal operations company building Go microservices that consume contract management, billing, or matter tracking services
target_users.rationale: SDK consumers are internal engineers; no external distribution

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Go module, not a hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Inter-service communication library with reliability and observability integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Protobuf code generation pipeline, Consul service discovery, circuit breaker state machine, and OTel trace propagation are non-trivial to integrate correctly

risk_level.rationale: Bugs in the SDK affect all consuming services simultaneously; changes require coordinated rollout

data_sensitivity.rationale: Transmits internal service-to-service payloads; not customer PII or financial data directly

external_exposure.rationale: Go module consumed only by internal services; not published to pkg.go.dev publicly

access_model.value: mTLS between all services using short-lived certificates issued by internal CA; SDK manages cert rotation transparently
access_model.rationale: Zero-trust service mesh posture; no service calls succeed without valid mTLS certificate

feature_scope.value: Typed client stubs for ContractManagementService, BillingService, and MatterTrackingService; Consul-based service discovery with health-check filtering; circuit breaker per service (closed/open/half-open); per-call retry with exponential backoff and jitter; OpenTelemetry span creation and W3C trace context propagation; mTLS transport with cert rotation
feature_scope.rationale: Full set of reliability and observability primitives needed to replace ad-hoc HTTP clients

mvp_scope.value: ContractManagementService client stub, Consul discovery, circuit breaker, and OTel tracing; BillingService and MatterTrackingService stubs in v1.1
mvp_scope.rationale: ContractManagementService has the most consumers and highest priority

security_baseline.value: mTLS required for all service calls; client certificates issued by internal CFSSL CA with 24-hour TTL; secrets never logged
security_baseline.rationale: Zero-trust inter-service communication policy

frontend_stack.value: Not applicable; library package only
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with google.golang.org/grpc, buf.build Protobuf toolchain, go-circuit-breaker, OpenTelemetry Go SDK
backend_stack.rationale: gRPC for typed inter-service calls; buf for Protobuf management; OTel for tracing

data_platform.value: Not applicable; SDK has no persistent storage; consuming services own their data
data_platform.rationale: Library pattern; no database dependency

hosting_target.value: Internal Go module registry (Artifactory Go proxy) with semantic versioning
hosting_target.rationale: Private module proxy prevents dependency on public internet for internal SDK

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK does not persist data; consuming services govern their own retention policies
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library has no storage layer

future_scope.value: Additional service stubs as platform grows (DocumentStorageService, NotificationService); gRPC-Web gateway client for browser consumers; load-balancing strategy pluggability
future_scope.rationale: Platform will add services over time; SDK must be extensible

constraints.value: Zero external runtime dependencies beyond Go stdlib and explicitly approved vendor libraries; backwards-compatible API changes only after v1.0.0; must pass go vet and golangci-lint in CI
constraints.rationale: Consuming services must be able to upgrade patch versions without code changes

observability_baseline.value: OpenTelemetry traces exported to Jaeger; SDK emits span attributes for service name, method, circuit breaker state, and retry count
observability_baseline.rationale: Distributed tracing is the primary observability primitive for inter-service call analysis

execution_preference.value: Library-driven development with buf.build schema registry as source of truth; consuming teams can pin SDK minor versions independently
execution_preference.rationale: Decoupled version management needed across 8 consuming services

project.goal: Typed, reliable inter-service communication SDK for legal ops platform microservices
project.target_users: Internal Go microservice engineers
project.timeline: v1.0 in 6 weeks, v1.1 in 10 weeks

security.auth: mTLS via CFSSL-issued 24-hour certs
security.secrets: certs loaded from filesystem path, never hardcoded

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: go vet, golangci-lint, go test ./..., buf lint, buf breaking
devops.hosting: Artifactory Go proxy for internal module distribution
devops.environments: dev (local replace directive), staging (Artifactory pre-release tags), production (semver tags)

reasoner_reviews.product: The SDK directly addresses the platform's pain point of duplicated, inconsistent HTTP client code across eight microservices. Prioritising ContractManagementService for v1.0 MVP is correct since it has the most callers and the highest blast radius from reliability issues. The typed Protobuf client stub approach gives consuming teams compile-time safety when service contracts change.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Using buf.build as the Protobuf schema registry and source of truth for code generation is the right call over manual .proto file copying. The circuit breaker must be per-service-instance, not global, to avoid cascade failures where BillingService degradation trips the ContractManagementService breaker. Consul health-check filtering in discovery ensures the SDK never routes to unhealthy instances.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS with 24-hour cert TTL is appropriate for zero-trust inter-service communication. The SDK must handle cert expiry gracefully by watching the cert file path and reloading the TLS config without dropping in-flight connections. Span attributes must never include request payloads to prevent sensitive matter data from appearing in Jaeger trace storage.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero unapproved external dependencies constraint is achievable since go-circuit-breaker and opentelemetry-go are already on the approved vendor list. The buf breaking change check in CI is essential to catch backwards-incompatible Protobuf changes before they break consuming services on the next SDK version upgrade.

backend.service_design: Package layout: legalops/sdk/contracts (ContractManagementClient), legalops/sdk/billing (BillingClient), legalops/sdk/matters (MatterTrackingClient), legalops/sdk/internal/discovery (Consul resolver), legalops/sdk/internal/breaker (circuit breaker), legalops/sdk/internal/transport (mTLS dialer)
backend.api_patterns: Each service package exposes a typed Client struct with methods mirroring the Protobuf service RPC definitions; constructor accepts functional options (WithConsulAddr, WithCircuitBreakerConfig, WithTracerProvider, WithCertPaths); gRPC interceptors chain handles retry, circuit breaker, and OTel span creation
backend.business_modules: buf.build code generation pipeline: .proto source in legalops/proto, buf.gen.yaml generates Go stubs into legalops/sdk/{service}/gen; circuit breaker state: closed (pass-through), open (fail-fast with ErrCircuitOpen), half-open (probe single request); Consul gRPC resolver implementing resolver.Builder interface for grpc.Dial
backend.failure_handling: Unary interceptor applies exponential backoff with jitter (base 100ms, max 2s, 3 retries) for codes.Unavailable and codes.ResourceExhausted; circuit breaker opens after 5 consecutive failures within a 10s window and probes after 30s half-open timeout; context cancellation propagated cleanly to abort in-flight retries
backend.scaling_notes: SDK is purely a client library; no server-side scaling concerns; Consul resolver caches endpoint lists and refreshes on health-check change events to reduce Consul query load
backend.backend_risks: Consul outage would prevent service discovery; mitigation: DNS fallback resolver with hardcoded service hostnames as last resort; proto schema divergence between SDK version and live service version causes RPC failures if buf breaking check is not enforced in service CI

security.auth_design: mTLS enforced via custom grpc.WithTransportCredentials using credentials.NewTLS with mutual TLS config loaded from cert and key paths; SDK watches cert file mtime and reloads TLS config on rotation without restarting the gRPC connection pool
security.secrets_management: Cert and key paths passed as WithCertPaths option; SDK never accepts inline PEM strings to prevent accidental secret logging; cert files managed by Vault Agent sidecar in consuming service pods
security.security_risks: Short-lived 24-hour cert TTL requires reliable cert rotation; if Vault Agent sidecar fails, certs expire and all SDK calls fail; consuming services must monitor cert expiry as a health check

data.entities: No persistent entities; SDK in-memory state: ServiceEndpointCache (Consul-resolved endpoints per service, TTL-refreshed), CircuitBreakerState (per service: state ENUM closed/open/half_open, failure_count, last_failure_at, probe_allowed_at), RetryAttemptCounter (per call context, cleared on success)
data.consistency_notes: Circuit breaker state is in-process memory per SDK instance; no distributed state sharing between instances of the same consuming service; this means circuit breaker opens independently per pod which is intentional for resilience isolation
data.data_risks: No cross-instance circuit breaker coordination means one degraded pod may trip its breaker while others continue routing to an unhealthy downstream; acceptable tradeoff for simplicity

devops.deployment_topology: Go module tagged with semver and pushed to internal Artifactory Go proxy; consuming services pin to minor version (require legalops/sdk v1.2.x); no runtime deployment
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on every PR and tag: go vet ./..., golangci-lint run, go test ./... -race -cover (coverage gate 85%), buf lint on .proto files, buf breaking --against main for backwards compat; on semver tag push: go mod tidy, module published to Artifactory via GOPROXY upload
devops.devops_risks: Artifactory Go proxy outage prevents consuming services from downloading new SDK versions; mitigation: consuming services vendor dependencies with go mod vendor in their CI for reproducible builds

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a Go inter-service communication SDK, not a hosted service, so all architectural decisions centre on the public API surface, reliability primitives, and the code generation pipeline. The core tradeoff is per-instance vs distributed circuit breaker state — choosing in-process state per SDK instance is simpler and avoids Redis dependency but means circuit breakers open independently per pod. The buf.build schema registry as source of truth for Protobuf, with breaking-change CI checks, is the critical mechanism preventing API drift between SDK versions and live services.
target_output.title: Legal Operations Platform Inter-Service Go SDK Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Go module SDK providing typed gRPC client stubs auto-generated from Protobuf definitions for ContractManagementService, BillingService, and MatterTrackingService within a distributed legal ops platform. The SDK integrates Consul service discovery with health-check filtering, a per-service in-process circuit breaker, exponential-backoff retry with jitter, mTLS transport with cert rotation, and OpenTelemetry trace propagation — replacing eight ad-hoc HTTP client implementations across the platform.
target_output.architecture_overview: Library-pattern Go module with no runtime deployment footprint. Package layout follows one sub-package per service (contracts, billing, matters) each exporting a typed Client struct, backed by shared internal packages for Consul-based gRPC name resolution, circuit breaker state machine, mTLS dialer with cert-file watching, and OTel unary interceptor. buf.build schema registry is the authoritative source for .proto files; buf.gen.yaml drives code generation into typed Go stubs committed to the SDK repo. Consuming services import the SDK as a Go module from the internal Artifactory proxy and wire clients via functional options. No global SDK state — each Client instance owns its circuit breaker and endpoint cache.
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22 with google.golang.org/grpc for RPC transport, buf.build CLI and buf.gen.yaml for Protobuf code generation, go-circuit-breaker for circuit breaker state machine, opentelemetry-go (go.opentelemetry.io/otel) with W3C trace context propagation, CFSSL internal CA for mTLS cert issuance, Consul gRPC resolver implementing gRPC name resolution API, Artifactory Go proxy for private module distribution, GitHub Actions CI with golangci-lint and buf breaking checks.
target_output.functional_feature_map: ContractManagementClient: typed methods for GetContract(ctx, ContractID) (*Contract, error), ListContracts(ctx, ListContractsRequest) (*ContractList, error), CreateAmendment(ctx, AmendmentRequest) (*Amendment, error). BillingClient: GetInvoice, ListOutstandingInvoices, RecordPayment with matter-linked billing IDs. MatterTrackingClient: GetMatter, AssignAttorney, UpdateMatterStatus with ABA matter-type enums. All clients: Consul-resolved endpoint balancing, circuit breaker fail-fast on open, OTel span per call, mTLS enforced.
target_output.system_components: ContractManagementClient package (typed stubs, functional options constructor). BillingClient package. MatterTrackingClient package. internal/discovery: ConsulResolver implementing grpc resolver.Builder; queries Consul health endpoint, filters passing instances, refreshes on change events. internal/breaker: CircuitBreaker struct with closed/open/half_open states; configurable failure threshold and probe timeout per service. internal/transport: mTLSDialer loading cert/key from filesystem paths with fsnotify watcher for rotation. internal/telemetry: UnaryInterceptor creating OTel spans with service, method, circuit_breaker_state, and retry_count attributes.
target_output.workflows: Service Call with Circuit Breaker: Consuming service calls contracts.Client.GetContract(ctx, id). SDK unary interceptor chain fires: OTel span created with traceparent propagated from ctx. Circuit breaker checked — if open, returns ErrCircuitOpen immediately. Consul resolver returns current healthy ContractManagementService endpoints. gRPC call dispatched over mTLS. On codes.Unavailable, retry interceptor applies exponential backoff (100ms base, 3 attempts max). On success, circuit breaker records success. On consecutive failures >= threshold, circuit breaker transitions to open state. Span closed with final status. Cert Rotation: Vault Agent sidecar writes new cert and key files to filesystem path. fsnotify watcher in internal/transport detects file modification. New tls.Config loaded from updated cert files. grpc.ClientConn UpdateState called to swap transport credentials without dropping connection pool.
target_output.data_model: In-process only — no persistent storage. ServiceEndpointCache: map[serviceName][]resolver.Address, refreshed on Consul health event or TTL expiry (30s). CircuitBreakerState per Client instance: {State ENUM closed/open/half_open, FailureCount int, LastFailureAt time.Time, ProbeAllowedAt time.Time, Config {FailureThreshold 5, OpenDuration 30s}}. RetryState per call: {Attempt int, LastBackoff time.Duration}. OTel span attributes: legalops.service STRING, legalops.method STRING, legalops.circuit_breaker_state STRING, legalops.retry_attempt INT.
target_output.api_design: Public Go API (not HTTP). contracts.NewClient(opts ...Option) (*ContractManagementClient, error) — options: WithConsulAddr(string), WithCircuitBreakerConfig(BreakerConfig), WithTracerProvider(trace.TracerProvider), WithCertPaths(cert, key, ca string). ContractManagementClient.GetContract(ctx context.Context, id ContractID) (*contractsv1.Contract, error). ContractManagementClient.Close() error — drains gRPC connection pool. Error types: ErrCircuitOpen (caller should back off), ErrServiceUnavailable (after all retries exhausted), ErrCertExpired (cert rotation failed). billing.NewClient and matters.NewClient follow identical constructor pattern.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS enforced via tls.RequireAndVerifyClientCert; SDK rejects any gRPC dial without cert paths configured. CFSSL-issued 24-hour TTL certs rotated by Vault Agent sidecar; fsnotify-based cert watcher reloads tls.Config on rotation event. OTel span attributes explicitly exclude request payload fields — only service name, method name, circuit breaker state, and retry count are recorded to prevent matter or contract data from appearing in Jaeger. golangci-lint with gosec linter in CI catches accidental credential logging patterns. No SDK secrets beyond cert paths passed as filesystem references.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Module versioned with semver tags (v1.0.0, v1.0.1, etc.) and published to internal Artifactory GOPROXY on tag push via GitHub Actions. Consuming services add require legalops/sdk v1.x.x to go.mod and set GOPROXY=https://artifactory.internal/api/go/go-virtual. go mod vendor in consuming service CI ensures reproducible builds even if Artifactory is unavailable. buf breaking check on every PR against main branch blocks Protobuf backwards-incompatible changes. Patch releases are backwards compatible by policy; minor releases may add new service client packages; major releases (v2+) only for breaking API changes with 90-day deprecation notice.
target_output.observability: Each SDK call creates an OTel span named legalops.{service}.{method} with trace context propagated via W3C traceparent header in gRPC metadata. Spans exported to Jaeger via the consuming service's configured TracerProvider. Recommended consuming-service dashboard: p50/p95/p99 latency per service+method pair, circuit breaker open events per service (derived from ErrCircuitOpen error rate), retry rate per service (legalops.retry_attempt > 0 filter), mTLS cert expiry countdown metric emitted as a gauge from the transport watcher.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost for the SDK itself — it is a library. Artifactory Go proxy storage cost is negligible for a Go module. Consul query load scales with number of consuming service instances; SDK endpoint cache with 30s TTL and event-driven refresh keeps Consul query rate proportional to deployment churn, not call volume. Circuit breaker in-process state avoids Redis dependency and associated cost.
target_output.phased_implementation: v1.0 (6 weeks): ContractManagementClient with Consul discovery, circuit breaker, OTel tracing, mTLS; buf.build pipeline; golangci-lint CI; Artifactory publishing; 85% test coverage including circuit breaker state machine unit tests and Consul resolver integration tests against a test Consul agent. v1.1 (4 weeks after v1.0): BillingClient and MatterTrackingClient stubs; functional options parity; integration test suite expanded to cover all three service clients against mock gRPC servers. v1.2 (future): Pluggable load-balancing strategy interface; gRPC-Web gateway client variant for browser consumers; additional service stubs as platform grows.
target_output.development_guidelines: All exported types and functions must have godoc comments. Public API changes require ADR and changelog entry before merge. buf breaking check enforced in CI — no manual overrides. golangci-lint with gosec, errcheck, and staticcheck enabled. Test files use testify/assert; table-driven tests for circuit breaker state transitions covering all closed→open→half_open→closed paths. go test -race required to pass before merge; data race in circuit breaker state would be a critical regression.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Consul service discovery outage prevents all inter-service calls for services without cached endpoints. Mitigation: DNS fallback resolver with stable service hostnames as last resort; endpoint cache persists across Consul blips for 30s TTL. Risk: SDK bug affects all eight consuming services simultaneously. Mitigation: staged rollout via minor version pinning — consuming services adopt new minor versions independently; canary deployment at one service before fleet-wide upgrade. Tradeoff: in-process circuit breaker state vs distributed (Redis-backed) — chose in-process for zero external dependency at the cost of independent per-pod tripping behaviour. Tradeoff: code-generated stubs from buf.build vs hand-rolled typed wrappers — code generation wins for correctness and maintenance velocity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Consul over Kubernetes DNS for discovery to support multi-cluster routing and health-check filtering that pure DNS cannot provide. Chose in-process circuit breaker over distributed to eliminate Redis dependency in a library. Chose W3C traceparent over B3 headers for OTel propagation to align with OpenTelemetry specification. Chose Artifactory Go proxy over GitHub Packages for tighter access control and offline vendoring support.

metadata.notes: Go SDK for typed gRPC inter-service communication across legal ops platform microservices; Consul discovery, in-process circuit breaker, mTLS, OTel tracing.

profile.domain: Financial Crime Compliance - AML Sanctions Watchlist Pipeline

project_goal.value: High-volume data pipeline ingesting structured and unstructured entity records from OFAC, World-Check, and Refinitiv AML screening vendors, deduplicating entity records, resolving aliases, and loading a unified versioned sanctions watchlist into a financial crime compliance platform used by global banks
project_goal.rationale: Banks require a single authoritative sanctions watchlist with full entity alias resolution to power AML screening decisions

target_users.value: AML compliance teams and financial crime screening systems at global banks consuming the unified watchlist via internal API and direct ClickHouse queries
target_users.rationale: Both human analysts and automated screening engines are consumers

project_class.rationale: Batch data ingestion, transformation, deduplication, and loading pipeline; no user-facing UI

capabilities.rationale: Multi-vendor feed integration, batch deduplication jobs, ClickHouse analytics, and pipeline orchestration

complexity_level.rationale: Processes 40M+ entity records across three vendors daily; probabilistic deduplication at scale requires distributed Spark execution

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect deduplication or missed alias resolution could cause false negatives in AML screening with regulatory and financial penalties

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sanctions data is proprietary vendor content with strict licensing and access controls

external_exposure.rationale: Watchlist data is consumed only by internal compliance systems; no external exposure

access_model.value: IAM role-based access; Spark EMR execution role; ClickHouse access via internal service accounts with row-level security by sanctions_program
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access to proprietary vendor content and sensitive deduplication outputs

feature_scope.value: SFTP ingestion from three vendor feeds (OFAC SDN, World-Check full file, Refinitiv delta); canonical entity schema normalisation; probabilistic deduplication with name and identifier similarity clustering; alias graph resolution; versioned ClickHouse snapshot loading; audit trail of every pipeline run and entity change; reconciliation report comparing vendor coverage
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for authoritative unified watchlist maintenance

mvp_scope.value: OFAC SDN ingestion, canonical normalisation, exact and fuzzy name deduplication, ClickHouse loading with versioned snapshots; World-Check and Refinitiv integration in v1.1
mvp_scope.rationale: OFAC SDN is the highest-priority mandatory list for US-regulated banks

security_baseline.value: S3 bucket with SSE-KMS for vendor feed storage; EMR execution role with minimum S3 and ClickHouse permissions; ClickHouse TLS in transit; vendor SFTP credentials in AWS Secrets Manager
security_baseline.rationale: Proprietary vendor data requires encryption at rest and in transit with secret rotation

frontend_stack.value: None; pipeline only; reconciliation reports delivered as S3-hosted HTML via internal BI tooling
frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend component

backend_stack.value: Scala 3 with Apache Spark on AWS EMR Serverless; Airflow 2.8 on MWAA for orchestration; Redis 7 for deduplication candidate cache
backend_stack.rationale: Spark for distributed probabilistic deduplication at 40M+ record scale; Airflow for DAG orchestration and retry management

data_platform.value: ClickHouse on AWS EC2 (r6i.8xlarge cluster) for versioned watchlist; S3 with Iceberg tables for raw vendor feed archive and intermediate deduplication state
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse column-store for sub-second sanctions screening queries; Iceberg for time-travel audit access to historical entity states

hosting_target.value: AWS EMR Serverless for Spark jobs; MWAA for Airflow orchestration; all data in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: EMR Serverless eliminates cluster management; MWAA provides managed Airflow

privacy_retention_policy.value: Vendor feed raw files retained 7 years in S3 Glacier for regulatory audit; processed watchlist snapshots retained indefinitely for point-in-time query; pipeline run logs retained 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: FinCEN AML program regulations require 5-year record retention; 7-year buffer for exam preparation

future_scope.value: Near-real-time OFAC delta feed ingestion; ML-based alias disambiguation using embedding similarity; API layer exposing watchlist queries to downstream screening engine
future_scope.rationale: Regulatory pressure to reduce OFAC update propagation latency from 24h to under 1h

constraints.value: Pipeline must complete daily ingestion and deduplication within 4-hour SLA window; ClickHouse watchlist table must be available for screening queries 23.5h/day; vendor feed credentials rotated every 90 days
constraints.rationale: AML screening systems depend on fresh watchlist; downtime windows limited to maintenance

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for pipeline job metrics; Airflow task-level logs in CloudWatch; ClickHouse query performance tracked via system.query_log; PagerDuty alert on pipeline failure or SLA breach
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline failures must page on-call immediately given regulatory sensitivity

execution_preference.value: Airflow DAG-driven nightly batch with idempotent Spark jobs checkpointed to S3 for re-run safety
execution_preference.rationale: Idempotency critical for safe retry on partial failures

compliance_context.value: OFAC SDN compliance requirements, FinCEN AML program rules (31 CFR Part 1010), EU 6th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (6AMLD), SOC 2 Type II controls for data pipeline integrity
compliance_context.rationale: Multi-jurisdictional AML obligations for global bank deployment

project.goal: Unified versioned AML sanctions watchlist from OFAC, World-Check, and Refinitiv with probabilistic deduplication
project.target_users: AML compliance teams and automated screening systems
project.timeline: v1.0 OFAC only in 8 weeks; v1.1 all three vendors in 14 weeks

backend.deduplication: Spark MLlib MinHash LSH for candidate blocking; Jaro-Winkler + identifier matching for scoring

security.access: IAM roles with least-privilege S3 and ClickHouse permissions

data.primary_store: ClickHouse r6i.8xlarge cluster for versioned watchlist snapshots
data.archive: S3 Iceberg tables for raw vendor feeds and dedup intermediate state
data.cache: Redis 7 for deduplication cluster candidate cache

devops.ci: GitLab CI: Scala build, unit tests, Spark integration tests against local Spark session
devops.environments: dev (local Spark), staging (EMR Serverless with synthetic vendor data), production

reasoner_reviews.product: The pipeline correctly prioritises OFAC SDN for v1.0 since US-regulated banks face immediate penalties for OFAC violations. The alias graph resolution is the most valuable differentiator over simple name matching — it catches sanctioned entities that have changed corporate names or operate through subsidiaries.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: MinHash LSH blocking for deduplication candidate generation is the right approach to avoid O(n^2) pairwise comparisons at 40M records. The Jaro-Winkler scoring threshold needs calibration on a held-out labelled set before production; the round 1 plan used a fixed threshold of 0.85 that generated excessive false-positive clusters for common Arabic and Chinese name transliterations. Iceberg time-travel on raw feed archives enables point-in-time audit queries without maintaining separate audit tables.
reasoner_reviews.security: The round 1 plan did not specify row-level security on ClickHouse by sanctions_program — this is a gap since OFAC data has different licensing and access restrictions than World-Check. ClickHouse row policies must partition access by sanctions_program_code so consuming systems only see lists they are licensed for. S3 bucket policy must deny direct access to raw vendor feed files except from the designated EMR execution role.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 4-hour SLA for 40M+ records is achievable with EMR Serverless auto-scaling to 200 vCPUs, but requires the MinHash LSH blocking step to run first and reduce the scoring workload by 98%+ before Jaro-Winkler scoring is applied. Checkpointing deduplication state to S3 Iceberg after blocking ensures partial failures can resume from blocking output rather than re-running the full Spark job.

backend.service_design: Airflow DAG aml_watchlist_nightly with tasks: ingest_vendor_feeds (SFTP download to S3), validate_feed_checksums, normalize_to_canonical_schema (Spark), run_dedup_blocking (Spark MinHash LSH), run_dedup_scoring (Spark Jaro-Winkler + identifier), resolve_alias_graph (Spark GraphX connected components), load_clickhouse_snapshot (Spark ClickHouse connector), generate_reconciliation_report
backend.api_patterns: No external API; Spark jobs accept S3 input/output paths and run configuration as parameters passed from Airflow XCom; ClickHouse watchlist table queried directly by downstream screening engine via JDBC
backend.business_modules: Canonical schema normaliser maps OFAC SDN XML fields, World-Check CSV, and Refinitiv JSON to watchlist_entities schema; MinHash LSH generates candidate pairs with Jaccard similarity >0.5 on normalised name tokens; Jaro-Winkler scoring with identifier (LEI, ISIN, passport) exact-match bonus produces final dedup score; GraphX resolves transitive alias chains into consolidated entity clusters
backend.failure_handling: Airflow task retry with 3 attempts and 15-minute delay; Spark checkpointing to S3 after blocking step; if scoring step fails, DAG resumes from blocking output; ClickHouse load is transactional — new snapshot version only becomes visible after full load completes; failed runs send PagerDuty alert within 5 minutes
backend.backend_risks: Vendor feed format changes (e.g., OFAC SDN schema update) break normalisation; mitigation: schema validation step with explicit failure before normalisation runs; vendor change notification webhook subscriptions where available

security.auth_design: EMR Serverless execution IAM role with S3 GetObject/PutObject on vendor feed bucket and dedup intermediate bucket only; separate ClickHouse load role with INSERT on watchlist schema
security.secrets_management: SFTP credentials for OFAC, World-Check, and Refinitiv stored in AWS Secrets Manager with 90-day rotation; rotated credentials fetched at DAG runtime via boto3 Secrets Manager SDK; never stored in Airflow Variables or S3
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Airflow task logs to CloudWatch with task_id, run_id, vendor, record_count, and status; ClickHouse system.query_log captures every watchlist query with user, query_hash, and rows_read for access audit; pipeline_runs table records each DAG run with vendor coverage, entity counts before/after dedup, and SLA met flag
security.security_risks: ClickHouse exposure without row-level security allows consumers licensed for OFAC only to query World-Check records; must implement ClickHouse row policies partitioned by sanctions_program_code before v1.0 launch

data.entities: watchlist_entities (entity_id UUID, canonical_name TEXT, entity_type ENUM individual/organization/vessel/aircraft, sanctions_program_code TEXT[], identifier_hashes TEXT[], alias_cluster_id UUID, source_vendor_codes TEXT[], snapshot_version UINT64, effective_date DATE, is_active BOOL). entity_aliases (alias_id UUID, entity_id FK, alias_name TEXT, alias_type ENUM primary/aka/fka/transliteration, language_code, source_vendor_code). deduplication_clusters (cluster_id UUID, member_entity_ids UUID[], confidence_score FLOAT, resolved_at TIMESTAMP, algorithm_version TEXT). pipeline_runs (run_id UUID, dag_run_id TEXT, started_at, completed_at, vendors_ingested TEXT[], entities_before_dedup INT, entities_after_dedup INT, new_aliases_discovered INT, sla_met BOOL).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse ReplacingMergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(effective_date) for watchlist_entities; snapshot_version column enables point-in-time queries without separate snapshot tables; S3 Iceberg table aml_raw_feeds stores original vendor files partitioned by vendor/date for time-travel audit access; Redis sorted set caches top-N MinHash candidate pairs during scoring step to reduce Spark shuffle volume
data.data_risks: Deduplication false negatives (missed alias clusters) are higher-risk than false positives; tuning strategy: conservative threshold during calibration, then tighten using precision-recall on labelled held-out set; vendor feed schema changes must trigger automated validation alert before processing proceeds

devops.deployment_topology: MWAA Airflow 2.8 in us-east-1 running nightly DAG; EMR Serverless application for Spark jobs (auto-scales 2-200 vCPUs); ClickHouse cluster on r6i.8xlarge (primary + 1 replica) in same VPC; S3 buckets in us-east-1 with versioning and MFA delete
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: sbt compile, sbt test (unit tests with Spark local mode), sbt assembly to fat JAR; on main merge: upload JAR to S3 deployment bucket; Airflow DAG file synced to MWAA S3 DAG bucket via GitLab deploy stage; staging smoke test runs DAG against synthetic vendor feed files
devops.observability_stack: Grafana dashboard: entity_count_by_vendor_post_dedup (gauge), dedup_cluster_size_distribution (histogram), pipeline_duration_minutes (gauge vs 240m SLA line), SFTP_download_bytes_by_vendor; Prometheus scrape from custom Spark metrics emitter; PagerDuty P1 on dag_failure or pipeline_duration > 210m (early warning before SLA breach)
devops.devops_risks: EMR Serverless cold start adds 3-5 minutes to job launch; pre-warm strategy: keep-alive worker pool during nightly window; ClickHouse replica lag during snapshot load should not exceed 30s or screening queries may read stale data

ISS-001.title: ClickHouse lacks row-level security by sanctions_program_code
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify ClickHouse row policies; consuming systems licensed for OFAC only can query World-Check and Refinitiv records, violating vendor licensing agreements

history.note: Identified in security review; row policies must partition access before launch

ISS-002.title: Jaro-Winkler threshold not calibrated for non-Latin name transliterations
ISS-002.detail: Fixed threshold of 0.85 generates excessive false-positive alias clusters for Arabic and Chinese name transliterations, causing legitimate distinct entities to be merged

history.note: Identified in architect review; threshold calibration step required before production scoring

Introduce script-aware Jaro-Winkler threshold configuration with separate thresholds for Latin, Arabic, and CJK character sets
Specify MinHash LSH blocking step checkpointing to S3 Iceberg to enable resume-from-blocking on Spark job failure

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity unresolved issues blocking production readiness

previous_audits.summary: Strong pipeline architecture overall but two high-severity gaps block production deployment: missing ClickHouse row-level security by vendor license scope, and uncalibrated Jaro-Winkler threshold that will cause false-positive entity merges at scale.
strengths: MinHash LSH blocking approach correctly reduces O(n^2) pairwise comparison problem
Iceberg time-travel for raw feed archive enables point-in-time regulatory audit
EMR Serverless auto-scaling design is appropriate for variable nightly job load
concerns: ClickHouse access control does not enforce vendor licensing boundaries
Deduplication scoring threshold is a placeholder not validated against labelled entity data
No explicit checkpoint strategy described for Spark job resume on partial failure
blocking_issues: ClickHouse row-level security by sanctions_program_code required before any multi-vendor data is loaded
Jaro-Winkler threshold calibration process must be defined before production scoring runs on live vendor data
recommendations: Implement ClickHouse row policies: CREATE ROW POLICY ofac_only ON watchlist_entities FOR SELECT USING sanctions_program_code = 'OFAC_SDN' TO ofac_readers_role
Define threshold calibration workflow using 1000-record labelled entity set; expose per-script-family thresholds as pipeline configuration
Add Spark checkpoint after MinHash LSH blocking step; document resume procedure in runbook

previous_plan.title: AML Sanctions Watchlist Ingestion Pipeline v1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Nightly Airflow-orchestrated Spark pipeline ingesting OFAC SDN, World-Check, and Refinitiv vendor feeds via SFTP, normalising to a canonical entity schema, deduplicating using MinHash LSH blocking and Jaro-Winkler scoring, and loading versioned snapshots into ClickHouse for downstream AML screening.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Airflow DAG triggers EMR Serverless Spark jobs sequentially: SFTP download to S3, normalisation, MinHash LSH blocking, Jaro-Winkler scoring with fixed 0.85 threshold, alias graph resolution via GraphX, ClickHouse snapshot load.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: S3 SSE-KMS for vendor feeds, EMR IAM roles, ClickHouse TLS in transit. OFAC and FinCEN compliance noted. Row-level access control not specified. Vendor credential rotation in Secrets Manager.
previous_plan.data_model: watchlist_entities, entity_aliases, deduplication_clusters tables in ClickHouse. No row policy definitions. Dedup scoring threshold fixed at 0.85.

target_output.thinking_summary: This revision must address two high-severity issues from round 1: missing ClickHouse row-level security by sanctions_program_code (ISS-001) and an uncalibrated Jaro-Winkler threshold causing false-positive entity merges for non-Latin name sets (ISS-002). The fix for ISS-001 is specific: CREATE ROW POLICY statements in ClickHouse partitioning access by sanctions_program_code column, enforced at the database layer. For ISS-002, the fix is to replace the fixed 0.85 threshold with a per-script-family configuration (Latin, Arabic, CJK) calibrated on a labelled entity set, surfaced as a pipeline configuration parameter rather than a hardcoded constant.
