target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 revision targeting ISS-002 (cache key normalization) and ISS-003 (per-provider circuit breaker). ISS-002 fix: canonical_key() function normalizes identity fields to deterministic form before SHA-256 hash, preventing collisions across name variants and partner boundaries. ISS-003 fix: Redis-backed circuit breaker state shared across ECS tasks, preventing fan-out attempts to known-failed providers and isolating provider failures from healthy request latency.

fix_report.action_taken: Added canonical_key() normalization function in cache module that uppercases ASCII name, converts DOB to YYYY-MM-DD, normalizes phone to E.164, and trims whitespace before SHA-256 hashing; documented in data_model and system_components sections
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cache hits are consistent across name casing and DOB format variants; no cross-partner cache collisions for distinct identity inputs

fix_report.action_taken: Specified per-provider circuit breaker using Redis HASH keyed by provider name, tracking failure_count, state (closed/open/half_open), and opened_at; threshold 10 failures within 60s triggers open state; 30s open window then single probe in half-open; all ECS tasks share breaker state via same Redis key
fix_report.expected_outcome: Experian or other provider failures are isolated within 10 failed requests; unaffected provider calls continue within normal latency range

target_output.title: KYC/KYB Verification Gateway - Round 3
target_output.executive_summary: Round 3 revision of the Rust/Axum KYC/KYB identity verification API. Addresses cache key normalization (ISS-002) by introducing a canonical_key() function that normalizes identity fields before SHA-256 hashing, eliminating cross-variant cache collisions. Resolves provider circuit breaker gap (ISS-003) with a Redis-backed per-provider circuit breaker shared across all ECS tasks, isolating individual provider failures from the overall request latency.
target_output.architecture_overview: Axum 0.7 HTTP server on Tokio runtime receives POST /v1/kyc and /v1/kyb verification requests from fintech partners authenticated via mTLS and API key. The VerificationOrchestrator first computes a canonical_key() from normalized identity fields (uppercase name, ISO-8601 DOB, E.164 phone) and checks Redis for a cached VerificationResult with 24h TTL. On cache miss, the orchestrator fans out to up to three provider clients (Jumio, Onfido, Experian) concurrently on Tokio, subject to per-provider Redis-backed circuit breakers. Providers with open circuit breakers are skipped; the consensus algorithm requires 2 responses from closed-state providers. Provider responses aggregate into a composite risk_score via ConsensusEngine. Results persist to PostgreSQL 16 RDS and are cached under the canonical key. Datadog APM spans trace each provider call and consensus computation.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Rust 1.77 with Axum 0.7 and Tokio 1.x async runtime. Auth: rustls for mTLS, tower middleware for API key validation. HTTP clients: reqwest with per-provider timeout. Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster with redis-rs async client. Database: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with sqlx. Serialization: serde_json. Tracing: tracing crate + opentelemetry-datadog exporter. Hosting: ECS Fargate, NLB for mTLS passthrough, ALB for HTTP. CI: GitHub Actions with cargo test + cargo clippy. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager.
target_output.functional_feature_map: POST /v1/kyc: synchronous individual identity verification with cache lookup, provider fan-out, consensus scoring, and result persistence. POST /v1/kyb: business entity verification aggregating Experian business registry check with Onfido document verification. GET /v1/verifications/{id}: retrieval of persisted verification record by ID. Webhook delivery: async result callback to partner-registered URL for 202 Accepted responses when provider fan-out exceeds SLA. Circuit breaker dashboard: GET /v1/admin/providers/health returns per-provider circuit breaker state. GET /v1/analytics/partner/{id}: verification volume, cache hit rate, and latency percentiles for partner SLA reporting.
target_output.system_components: NLB (mTLS TCP passthrough) -> Axum HTTP server (ECS Fargate tasks) -> tower middleware stack (mTLS cert validation, API key extraction, rate limiter). VerificationOrchestrator: canonical_key() normalization -> Redis cache lookup -> per-provider circuit breaker check (Redis HASH kyc:circuit:{provider}) -> Tokio fan-out to ProviderClient impls (JumioClient, OnfidoClient, ExperianClient) with 1.5s timeout each -> ConsensusEngine (2-of-3 majority with weighted scoring). PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for durable verification_requests and provider_responses. Redis 7 ElastiCache cluster for result cache (24h TTL) and circuit breaker state. AWS Secrets Manager for provider API credentials. Datadog APM sidecar per ECS task.
target_output.workflows: KYC Verification (cache hit): Partner POSTs /v1/kyc with identity fields; Axum middleware validates mTLS cert and API key; canonical_key() normalizes name/dob/phone and computes SHA-256 hash; Redis GET returns cached VerificationResult; 200 response returned in <10ms with cache_hit=true. KYC Verification (cache miss with full fan-out): canonical_key hash misses Redis; orchestrator reads per-provider circuit breaker state from Redis HASH; skips providers with open breakers; fans out to remaining providers concurrently via Tokio spawn_set; each provider call enforces 1.5s timeout; ConsensusEngine aggregates 2+ responses into composite risk_score; result written to PostgreSQL verification_requests and provider_responses; cached in Redis under canonical key with 24h TTL; response returned in typically 100-180ms. Provider Circuit Breaker Trip: provider returns 5xx or times out; Rust circuit breaker increments Redis HINCRBY kyc:circuit:{provider} failure_count; after 10 failures within 60s rolling window, sets state to open and records opened_at; subsequent requests skip that provider; after 30s, single probe request in half-open state; success resets to closed; failure re-opens.
target_output.data_model: verification_requests (id UUID PK, partner_id UUID FK, subject_type ENUM individual/business, canonical_key_hash VARCHAR, risk_score NUMERIC, decision ENUM approved/declined/review, cache_hit BOOLEAN, providers_used TEXT[], created_at TIMESTAMP). identity_profiles (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, name_encrypted BYTEA, dob_encrypted BYTEA, id_number_encrypted BYTEA, address_encrypted BYTEA, eu_subject_flag BOOLEAN, biometric_score_encrypted BYTEA, biometric_purged_at TIMESTAMP). provider_responses (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, provider ENUM jumio/onfido/experian, raw_score NUMERIC, status ENUM success/timeout/error, response_ms INT, fetched_at TIMESTAMP). risk_scores (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, composite_score NUMERIC, provider_scores_json JSONB, algorithm_version VARCHAR). adverse_media_hits (id UUID PK, request_id UUID FK, source VARCHAR, excerpt_encrypted BYTEA, hit_date DATE). canonical_key normalization: uppercase_ascii(name) + iso8601(dob) + e164(phone) -> SHA-256 hex string -> Redis key prefix kyc:result:{hash}.
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/kyc body: {name, dob, id_number, address, phone, id_doc_type} -> 200 {verification_id, risk_score, decision, cache_hit, providers_used} or 202 {verification_id, webhook_url} if fan-out >1.8s. POST /v1/kyb body: {business_name, registration_number, jurisdiction, ultimate_beneficial_owners[]} -> 200 {verification_id, risk_score, decision, entity_match_score}. GET /v1/verifications/{id} -> 200 {verification_id, risk_score, decision, created_at, providers_used}. POST /v1/webhooks/register body: {url, events[]} -> 201 {webhook_id}. GET /v1/admin/providers/health -> 200 [{provider, state, failure_count, opened_at}]. GET /v1/analytics/partner/{partner_id}?from=&to= -> 200 {total_verifications, cache_hit_rate, p50_ms, p99_ms}. Auth: mTLS cert + X-API-Key header on all routes.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS: NLB TCP passthrough; Axum rustls extractor validates client cert CN against partner_registry PostgreSQL table; rejects unknown CNs with 401. API key: partner_keys Redis HASH lookup on each request for low-latency validation; key scoped to allowed operations. PII encryption: AES-256-GCM with per-tenant keys from AWS Secrets Manager for name_encrypted, dob_encrypted, id_number_encrypted fields. No PII in structured logs - only verification_id, risk_score, and cache_hit boolean. Biometric purge: PostgreSQL cron job runs nightly, sets biometric_score_encrypted=NULL and biometric_purged_at for records older than 30 days. GDPR erasure: eu_subject_flag triggers erasure job replacing all encrypted fields with tombstone BYTEA('ERASED') within 72h; erasure logged to audit_events. FinCEN CDD: verification_requests retained immutably for 7 years; deletion attempts on CDD records blocked at application layer.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: cargo test (unit + integration with test PostgreSQL container), cargo clippy --deny warnings, cargo audit for dependency vulnerabilities on every PR; Docker multi-stage build (builder + distroless runtime) on merge to main; ECR push with image tag = git SHA. Deployment: ECS rolling update with health check on GET /health returning circuit breaker summary; new task must pass 3 consecutive health checks before old task termination. Blue/green with CodeDeploy for major releases. Environments: dev (localstack + test Redis), staging (real providers in test mode, ElastiCache, RDS), production (dual region us-east-1 + eu-west-1 with Route53 latency routing). Secrets: Secrets Manager with automatic 60-day rotation for provider API keys; ACM Private CA for partner mTLS certificates with 1-year validity.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM: trace per verification request spanning mTLS validation, cache lookup, provider fan-out, and consensus computation. Custom metrics: verification_latency_ms (histogram by subject_type and cache_hit), cache_hit_rate (gauge per partner), provider_response_ms (histogram per provider), circuit_breaker_state (gauge: 0=closed, 1=half_open, 2=open per provider). PagerDuty: p99 latency >500ms (5-minute window), error rate >0.5% (2-minute window), any circuit breaker open >5 minutes, any provider in open state during peak hours. Datadog SLO tracking 99.9% availability against 200 OK response rate. CloudWatch alarms for ECS task OOM and RDS connection pool exhaustion.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate (4 tasks, 2 vCPU each) ~$400/month. ElastiCache r6g.large cluster ~$250/month. RDS db.r6g.large Multi-AZ ~$350/month. Secrets Manager + data transfer ~$50/month. Total ~$1,100/month at 50K verifications/day. Cache hit rate of 30% reduces Jumio/Onfido provider call costs by roughly $8K/month at volume pricing. ECS auto-scaling on ALB RequestCountPerTarget 500 req/task handles 10x load spikes without pre-provisioning.
target_output.phased_implementation: Round 3 deliverables (current sprint): canonical_key() normalization function with unit tests for name casing, DOB format, and E.164 phone variants; Redis-backed per-provider circuit breaker with integration test simulating Experian failure isolation. Remaining Phase 1 work: KYB business entity endpoint, async webhook delivery for 202 Accepted flows. Phase 2: partner analytics API, ongoing monitoring post-verification, adverse media endpoint.
target_output.development_guidelines: Rust: no unwrap() in production paths; all provider errors mapped to typed ProviderError enum before ConsensusEngine. canonical_key() must have unit tests covering: uppercase name normalization, multiple DOB format inputs, E.164 phone normalization, empty optional fields. Circuit breaker state transitions must have integration tests with mock Redis. cargo audit in CI blocks merge on RUSTSEC advisories with CVSS >7. Load test baseline: 1,000 concurrent verification requests with 100% cache miss must complete p50 <200ms before each release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk (ISS-002 resolved): canonical_key() normalization now prevents cache collisions across name variants and DOB formats; input normalization unit tests enforce correctness. Risk (ISS-003 resolved): Redis-backed circuit breaker isolates individual provider failures; shared state across ECS tasks prevents partial fan-out to open-circuit providers. Remaining risk: Redis cluster failure would disable both result caching and circuit breaker state, forcing all requests to live provider calls; mitigation: Redis cluster mode with 2 replicas and ElastiCache automatic failover within 60s. Tradeoff: shared Redis circuit breaker state adds ~1ms to each request for HGET; acceptable versus latency impact of fan-out to failed provider.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed canonical_key() scope: normalizes name, DOB, phone; does not include address (too many format variants). Confirmed Redis as circuit breaker state store (shared across ECS tasks vs. in-process state per task). Partner mTLS certificate revocation via ACM Private CA CRL confirmed. Biometric purge schedule confirmed as nightly cron at 02:00 UTC.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision resolving ISS-002 (cache key normalization) and ISS-003 (per-provider circuit breaker) for sub-200ms KYC/KYB verification gateway.

profile.domain: Contract Lifecycle Management Migration

project_goal.value: Ruby gem for migrating contract data from legacy Salesforce CPQ contract objects to Ironclad CLM, providing configurable field transformers, relationship graph traversal for dependent clauses, and idempotent re-run support
project_goal.rationale: Enterprise legal teams replacing CPQ with dedicated CLM need a programmatic migration path that survives interrupted runs

target_users.value: Salesforce admins and legal technology consultants at enterprise legal teams migrating from Salesforce CPQ to Ironclad CLM
target_users.rationale: Technical operators running migrations on behalf of enterprise clients

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a RubyGem artifact; no hosted service component

capabilities.rationale: Pure migration library with Salesforce and Ironclad API integrations; no UI

complexity_level.rationale: Relationship graph traversal and idempotent checkpoint logic are non-trivial but bounded in scope

risk_level.rationale: Confidential contract data in transit between systems; migration errors could create duplicate or corrupted CLM records

data_sensitivity.rationale: Contract records including pricing, obligations, and counterparty data are commercially confidential

external_exposure.rationale: Gem runs in the consultant's local or CI environment; no public endpoints

access_model.value: OAuth 2.0 client credentials for Salesforce Connected App; Ironclad API key; all credentials injected via environment variables
access_model.rationale: Standard OAuth for Salesforce; Ironclad uses API key authentication

feature_scope.value: Salesforce CPQ contract object graph traversal, configurable field mapping YAML definitions, amendment and renewal relationship traversal, Ironclad workflow schema creation, idempotent checkpoint file, migration dry-run mode, per-contract error log
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration lifecycle from CPQ extraction through Ironclad import with re-run safety

mvp_scope.value: Parent contract and amendment record extraction, YAML field mapping, Ironclad workflow creation, JSON checkpoint file for idempotent re-run
mvp_scope.rationale: Core migration path for the most common CPQ contract structure

security_baseline.value: No credentials in gem code or checkpoint files; OAuth tokens stored only in memory; contract field values not logged at any level
security_baseline.rationale: Contract data is commercially sensitive; tokens must not persist to disk

frontend_stack.value: none
frontend_stack.rationale: Library SDK with no UI

backend_stack.value: Ruby 3.3
backend_stack.rationale: Salesforce consultants predominantly use Ruby tooling; faraday gem for HTTP clients

data_platform.value: No managed database; checkpoint state persisted to JSON file on local filesystem
data_platform.rationale: Must run without infrastructure dependencies; JSON checkpoint file is portable

hosting_target.value: RubyGems.org
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Ruby gem distribution

privacy_retention_policy.value: Checkpoint files contain only contract IDs and status hashes; no contract content persisted; consultants instructed to delete checkpoint files after successful migration
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimisation; contract content must not persist beyond migration run

future_scope.value: Icertis and ContractWorks source adapters, Ironclad bulk import optimisation for migrations above 10k contracts, GUI progress reporter
future_scope.rationale: Expand to other source CLM systems after Salesforce CPQ path is proven

constraints.value: Must run on Ruby 3.1+; no native extensions; gem install size under 500KB; Salesforce API governor limits require batch-aware request throttling
constraints.rationale: Target environments may restrict native gem compilation; Salesforce API limits 10k calls per 24 hours for Connected Apps

observability_baseline.value: Ruby Logger at DEBUG/INFO/ERROR levels; per-contract migration status written to structured migration_log.json; contract field values excluded from all log lines
observability_baseline.rationale: Consultants need detailed migration logs for post-migration reconciliation; no sensitive data in logs

execution_preference.value: Sequential default with configurable concurrency via Concurrent Ruby thread pool for clients with higher API rate limits
execution_preference.rationale: Safe default for API governor limits; parallelism available when limits allow

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 5 data minimisation for EU counterparty data in contract records; attorney-client privilege for contracts with legal counsel; state bar confidentiality obligations for law firm clients
compliance_context.rationale: Enterprise contracts may include EU data subject counterparties and privileged legal terms

project.goal: Provide Salesforce admins with an idempotent Ruby gem for migrating CPQ contracts to Ironclad CLM
project.target_users: Salesforce admins and legal technology consultants
project.timeline: GA within 6 weeks of MVP acceptance

backend.api: No hosted API; programmatic Ruby API surface

security.auth: OAuth 2.0 via faraday-oauth2 for Salesforce; API key via environment variable for Ironclad
security.credential_handling: Tokens in memory only; no disk persistence; contract values excluded from logs

data.source: Salesforce SOQL queries via REST API
data.checkpoint: JSON file on local filesystem; keyed by Salesforce contract ID hash

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with RSpec and RuboCop
devops.publishing: RubyGems.org via gem push with OTP

open_questions.amendment_chain_depth: Some CPQ contracts have amendment chains of depth 8+; confirm recursive traversal is bounded

reasoner_reviews.product: The gem addresses a real pain point — Salesforce CPQ contract objects are deeply nested with amendment chains that manual migration cannot handle reliably. The idempotent checkpoint design is the highest-value feature since enterprise migrations run over multiple days and are frequently interrupted. Round 1 established core structure; round 2 should close the amendment chain depth safety gap and improve error reporting granularity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The relationship graph traversal for CPQ amendment chains must handle cycles (CPQ data quality issues create circular amendment references) and configurable maximum depth to prevent runaway recursion. The YAML mapping engine should support transformer lambdas for complex field conversions such as CPQ status codes to Ironclad workflow states. Faraday with faraday-retry handles Salesforce 429 rate limit responses correctly if retry_options are configured with respect_retry_after_header=true.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth access tokens must be stored as frozen strings in memory and never assigned to instance variables that persist between contract batches. The checkpoint JSON file must contain only Salesforce contract ID hashes (SHA256 of the Salesforce ID) not raw Salesforce IDs, to avoid exposing object IDs in shared filesystem environments. Dry-run mode must not call Ironclad write endpoints.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Salesforce API governor limits of 10k calls per 24 hours require the traversal to batch SOQL queries using relationship queries rather than individual record fetches. A migration of 5k contracts requires approximately 6k API calls when amendment chains average 1.2 amendments per contract — within the daily limit with headroom.

backend.service_design: Public API: IroncladMigration::Runner (entry point), IroncladMigration::Config (builder: salesforce credentials, ironclad api key, mapping YAML path, checkpoint path, dry_run, concurrency, max_amendment_depth). Internal modules: Salesforce::Client (SOQL query execution with faraday-retry), Salesforce::ContractGraphTraverser (BFS traversal of amendment chains with cycle detection and depth limit), Mapping::Engine (YAML-driven field transformer pipeline), Ironclad::Client (workflow creation API calls), Checkpoint::Store (JSON file with SHA256-keyed records).
backend.api_patterns: Ruby block-based progress callbacks: runner.on_contract_migrated { |contract_id, ironclad_id| }. Runner.run returns MigrationReport (total, migrated, skipped, failed, error_log_path). Dry-run mode: Runner.dry_run executes full traversal and mapping but returns simulated MigrationReport without calling Ironclad write endpoints.
backend.failure_handling: Per-contract rescue block catches Salesforce API errors, Ironclad API errors, and mapping errors; logs to migration_log.json with contract SHA256 hash, error class, and message (no contract field values). Checkpoint.store marks failed contracts as FAILED with error_class; retry run skips MIGRATED records and retries FAILED records.
backend.backend_risks: CPQ amendment chains with circular references crash naive recursive traversal; Checkpoint::Store SHA256 keying is correct but requires SHA256 of Salesforce ID string not the contract content hash to remain stable across re-runs.

security.auth_design: Salesforce OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow via faraday-oauth2 middleware; token stored as frozen String in Salesforce::Client instance, not in Config object. Ironclad API key passed as Authorization header on each request via faraday middleware; not stored in instance variables between requests.
security.secrets_management: Config requires salesforce_client_id and salesforce_client_secret from ENV; raises IroncladMigration::ConfigError if absent. No credentials written to checkpoint file, migration log, or Ruby Logger output at any level.
security.privacy_controls: Checkpoint JSON stores SHA256(salesforce_contract_id) as key; no raw Salesforce IDs. Migration log stores only contract SHA256 hash, error class, and Ironclad workflow ID on success. Transformer lambdas must not capture contract field values in closures that persist to outer scope.

data.entities: ContractRecord (salesforce_id, contract_number, account_id, status, start_date, end_date, total_value, currency, contract_type). AmendmentRecord (salesforce_id, parent_contract_id FK, amendment_number, amendment_date, changed_fields HASH). ClauseRecord (salesforce_id, contract_id FK, clause_type, clause_text). MappingDefinition (source_field, target_field, transformer symbol or lambda). CheckpointRecord (sha256_key, salesforce_id_hash, ironclad_workflow_id, status ENUM migrated/failed/skipped, error_class, migrated_at).
data.migration_notes: SOQL batch query fetches parent contracts and their amendments in a single relationship query: SELECT Id, ContractNumber, (SELECT Id, AmendmentNumber FROM Amendments__r) FROM Contract WHERE Status != 'Draft'. Amendment chain traversal uses BFS queue with visited set keyed on Salesforce ID to detect cycles. Max_amendment_depth config param defaults to 10.
data.data_risks: CPQ custom object fields vary by Salesforce org configuration; mapping YAML must include a strict_mode option that raises on unmapped source fields to prevent silent data loss. Ironclad workflow schema version changes between API versions may silently drop fields; pin to Ironclad API version in faraday headers.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: bundle exec rspec (unit + integration tests with VCR cassettes for Salesforce and Ironclad API calls), bundle exec rubocop, gem build validation on PR. Tag-triggered release: bundle exec rake release pushes to RubyGems.org with OTP. VCR cassettes checked into test/fixtures/vcr_cassettes; sensitive fields in cassettes scrubbed via VCR filter_sensitive_data.
devops.backup_and_recovery: Checkpoint file is the migration state; consultants instructed to copy checkpoint file before each run. Resume-on-failure: Runner.run with existing checkpoint skips MIGRATED records and retries FAILED.

ISS-001.title: Amendment chain cycle detection missing from traversal
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 ContractGraphTraverser used recursive depth-first traversal with no visited set; CPQ data quality issues with circular amendment references cause infinite recursion and stack overflow.

history.note: Engineer flagged during code review of traversal module

ISS-002.title: Checkpoint file stores raw Salesforce IDs
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 checkpoint JSON used raw Salesforce 18-character IDs as keys, exposing internal object identifiers in shared filesystem environments.

history.note: Security reviewer flagged raw ID exposure
SHA256 keying implemented in Checkpoint::Store

focus_issues.title: Amendment chain cycle detection missing from traversal
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 ContractGraphTraverser used recursive depth-first traversal with no visited set; CPQ data quality issues with circular amendment references cause infinite recursion and stack overflow.

latest_recommendations: Replace recursive DFS with BFS queue and visited set keyed on Salesforce ID to detect and break cycles
Add max_amendment_depth config parameter defaulting to 10 as secondary safety bound
Add RSpec test with fixture that includes a circular amendment reference to verify cycle detection

previous_plan.title: Salesforce CPQ to Ironclad CLM Migration Gem — Initial Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: Ruby 3.3 gem providing Salesforce CPQ contract graph extraction, YAML-driven field mapping, and Ironclad workflow creation with JSON checkpoint idempotency and dry-run mode.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Pure library with three layers: Salesforce extraction (SOQL queries via faraday), YAML mapping engine, and Ironclad import client. Checkpoint JSON file persists migration state for re-run safety.
previous_plan.data_model: ContractRecord, AmendmentRecord, ClauseRecord, MappingDefinition, CheckpointRecord — all in-memory during migration run.

target_output.thinking_summary: ISS-001 requires replacing the recursive DFS amendment traversal with BFS using a visited set keyed on Salesforce ID. BFS naturally handles cycles by checking the visited set before enqueuing each amendment. A secondary max_amendment_depth bound catches extreme chain lengths from data quality issues. The fix also requires an RSpec test fixture with a circular amendment reference to prove cycle detection works in CI. ISS-002 (SHA256 keying) was resolved in round 1 and is preserved in the updated plan.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced Salesforce::ContractGraphTraverser recursive DFS with BFS queue implementation. Traversal maintains a visited Set keyed on Salesforce ID; before enqueuing each amendment, checks visited.include?(amendment.salesforce_id); if present, emits cycle_detected warning log and skips. Added max_amendment_depth config parameter (default 10) as secondary bound that halts traversal and emits depth_limit_reached warning for chains exceeding the limit. RSpec test added with VCR cassette fixture containing a circular amendment reference (Contract A → Amendment 1 → Amendment 2 → Contract A); test asserts migration completes without exception and cycle_detected appears in log output.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ContractGraphTraverser handles circular CPQ amendment references without stack overflow; cycle detection verified by automated RSpec test in CI

target_output.title: Salesforce CPQ to Ironclad CLM Migration Gem
target_output.executive_summary: Ruby 3.3 gem enabling Salesforce admins to migrate CPQ contract objects to Ironclad CLM with BFS amendment chain traversal with cycle detection, YAML-driven field mapping with transformer lambdas, idempotent JSON checkpoint re-run, and dry-run mode that validates mapping without calling Ironclad write endpoints. Handles amendment relationships of arbitrary depth with max_amendment_depth safety bound.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure library gem distributed via RubyGems.org with no hosted runtime. Three-layer internal design: extraction layer (Salesforce::Client issuing batched SOQL relationship queries; ContractGraphTraverser using BFS with visited set for cycle-safe amendment chain traversal), transformation layer (Mapping::Engine applying YAML field mapping definitions with optional transformer lambdas; strict_mode option for unmapped field detection), and import layer (Ironclad::Client posting to Workflow API with API version pinning). Runner orchestrates all layers, respects Salesforce API governor limits via faraday-retry with respect_retry_after_header=true, and writes progress to JSON checkpoint file keyed by SHA256 of Salesforce contract ID.
target_output.technology_stack: Ruby 3.3 with Bundler 2.5 for gem management. faraday 2.x with faraday-retry and faraday-oauth2 for Salesforce and Ironclad HTTP clients. concurrent-ruby for optional thread pool parallelism. psych for YAML mapping definition parsing. Ruby standard library digest for SHA256 checkpoint keying. RSpec 3.x with VCR for HTTP cassette recording. RuboCop with rubocop-rspec for style enforcement. No native extensions; gem size under 500KB.
target_output.functional_feature_map: CPQ Contract Extraction: batched SOQL relationship queries fetching parent contracts with nested amendments in a single API call per batch of 200. Amendment Graph Traversal: BFS with visited set keyed on Salesforce ID; max_amendment_depth secondary bound (configurable, default 10); cycle_detected and depth_limit_reached warnings emitted to Logger and migration_log.json. YAML Field Mapping: source_field to target_field with optional transformer symbol (:to_date, :upcase) or lambda for complex conversions; strict_mode raises on unmapped source fields. Ironclad Workflow Creation: POST to /v1/workflows with mapped field payload; pins to Ironclad API version v2025-01 via Accept-Version header. Idempotent Checkpoint: JSON file keyed by SHA256(salesforce_id); MIGRATED records skipped; FAILED records retried. Dry-Run Mode: full traversal and mapping executed; Ironclad write calls replaced with dry_run stubs returning simulated workflow IDs.
target_output.system_components: IroncladMigration::Runner (orchestrator; dispatches to traverser, mapper, importer; enforces sequencing; writes checkpoint after each contract batch). IroncladMigration::Config (builder validating required ENV credentials; raises ConfigError on missing salesforce_client_id, salesforce_client_secret, ironclad_api_key). Salesforce::Client (faraday connection with OAuth 2.0 client credentials middleware; token stored as frozen String; respects 429 Retry-After headers). Salesforce::ContractGraphTraverser (BFS queue with visited Set; max_amendment_depth configurable; emits cycle warnings). Mapping::Engine (YAML loader; transformer registry; strict_mode unmapped field detection). Ironclad::Client (faraday connection; Accept-Version: v2025-01; no credential storage between requests). Checkpoint::Store (JSON file read/write; SHA256 keyed; atomic file rename on write to prevent partial corruption).
target_output.workflows: Migration Run: consultant instantiates Config with ENV credentials and YAML mapping path; calls Runner.run. Runner queries Salesforce for all non-draft contracts in batches of 200 via SOQL relationship query. For each contract, ContractGraphTraverser performs BFS to collect parent contract and all amendments; visited set prevents cycles; depth limit halts extreme chains. Each contract record plus its amendment records passed to Mapping::Engine which applies YAML field transformers to produce Ironclad workflow payload. Ironclad::Client POSTs payload; on success, Checkpoint::Store writes {sha256_key: SHA256(sf_id), ironclad_workflow_id: id, status: migrated, migrated_at: iso8601}. On API error, FAILED record written with error_class; migration continues to next contract. Dry-Run Validation: Runner.dry_run performs identical flow except Ironclad::Client is replaced with DryRunStub that logs the payload and returns a simulated workflow ID; MigrationReport returned with dry_run: true and simulated_count.
target_output.data_model: ContractRecord (salesforce_id String, contract_number String, account_id String, account_name String, status String, start_date Date, end_date Date, total_value BigDecimal, currency String, contract_type String, amendments Array of AmendmentRecord). AmendmentRecord (salesforce_id String, parent_contract_id String, amendment_number Integer, amendment_date Date, changed_fields Hash). ClauseRecord (salesforce_id String, contract_id String, clause_type String, clause_text String). MappingDefinition (source_field String, target_field String, transformer Symbol or Proc, required Boolean). CheckpointRecord (sha256_key String, salesforce_id_hash String, ironclad_workflow_id String nullable, status Symbol migrated/failed/skipped, error_class String nullable, migrated_at String iso8601 nullable). MigrationReport (total Integer, migrated Integer, skipped Integer, failed Integer, dry_run Boolean, error_log_path String).
target_output.api_design: Public Ruby API: IroncladMigration::Config.build { |c| c.salesforce_client_id = ENV['SF_CLIENT_ID']; c.ironclad_api_key = ENV['IRONCLAD_KEY']; c.mapping_yaml = './mapping.yml'; c.checkpoint_path = './checkpoint.json'; c.dry_run = false; c.max_amendment_depth = 10; c.concurrency = 1 }. IroncladMigration::Runner.new(config).run returns MigrationReport. Runner.new(config).dry_run returns MigrationReport with dry_run: true. Callback registration: runner.on_contract_migrated { |sf_id_hash, ironclad_id| }; runner.on_contract_failed { |sf_id_hash, error| }; runner.on_cycle_detected { |sf_id, cycle_path| }. Exception hierarchy: ConfigError (missing credentials or mapping), SalesforceApiError (HTTP errors), IroncladApiError (HTTP errors), MappingError (strict_mode unmapped field).
target_output.security_and_compliance: OAuth tokens: Salesforce access token stored as frozen String inside Salesforce::Client instance; not assigned to Config or any module-level variable; eligible for GC between batch calls. Ironclad API key: retrieved from ENV at Config.build time, stored as frozen String in Config; passed via Authorization header by faraday middleware on each request; not retained in response objects. Checkpoint file: keyed by SHA256(salesforce_id) — raw Salesforce IDs never written to disk. Migration log: records SHA256 hash, error_class (if failed), and Ironclad workflow ID (on success) — no contract field values at any log level. Ruby Logger filter applied to all messages: pattern matching CPQ field value patterns (currency amounts, contract numbers) triggers WARN and redacts value. GDPR: EU counterparty data processed in memory during migration run only; consultant responsible for not persisting checkpoint beyond migration completion per documented gem README guidance.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: RubyGems.org distribution via bundle exec rake release requiring OTP code. GitHub Actions CI: bundle exec rubocop (zero offences gate), bundle exec rspec (unit tests, VCR integration tests with cassettes in test/fixtures/vcr_cassettes; sensitive fields scrubbed via VCR.configure filter_sensitive_data). Gem build validation step in CI checks gemspec and confirms no native extensions. Tag-triggered release workflow. Version strategy: semver; minor version for new source/target adapter support, patch for traversal and mapping fixes. VCR cassettes for Salesforce SOQL API responses and Ironclad Workflow API responses checked into repo; cassettes re-recorded annually or on API version upgrade.
target_output.observability: Ruby Logger with configurable level (default INFO; DEBUG logs BFS traversal steps). Structured migration_log.json written incrementally: {sha256_key, status, ironclad_workflow_id, error_class, migrated_at} per contract record; never contains contract field values. MigrationReport at run completion: total contracts processed, migrated count, skipped count (already migrated in checkpoint), failed count, cycles detected count, depth_limit_reached count, total_duration_seconds, error_log_path. Progress callbacks allow integrators to build CLI progress bars or CI log output using on_contract_migrated and on_contract_failed hooks.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No infrastructure cost; gem runs in the consultant's process. Salesforce API governor limit of 10k calls per 24 hours is the primary constraint. Batch SOQL relationship queries reduce API call count: 5k contracts with average 1.2 amendments requires approximately 6.2k API calls including pagination. With concurrency=1 (default) at Salesforce rate limit headroom, a 5k contract migration completes in approximately 90 minutes. Concurrency=4 possible for organisations with higher Connected App API limits; safeguarded by faraday-retry 429 backoff.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (4 weeks): Salesforce SOQL batch extraction, ContractGraphTraverser with BFS cycle detection and max_amendment_depth, YAML mapping engine with transformer registry, Ironclad Workflow API client, JSON checkpoint idempotency, dry-run mode, RubyGems publishing pipeline, VCR test fixtures including circular amendment fixture. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Clause record extraction and mapping, strict_mode unmapped field detection, amendment chain depth reporting in MigrationReport, Concurrent Ruby thread pool for configurable parallelism. Phase 3 (3 weeks): Icertis source adapter skeleton, bulk import optimisation for migrations above 10k contracts using Ironclad batch endpoint.
target_output.development_guidelines: Ruby 3.3 with frozen_string_literal: true in all files. RuboCop with rubocop-rspec; zero offences required to merge. RSpec with 90% coverage requirement on Salesforce::ContractGraphTraverser and Mapping::Engine modules. Required test cases: BFS traversal visits all amendments in correct order, cycle detection prevents infinite loop and emits warning, max_amendment_depth halts traversal and emits warning, strict_mode raises on unmapped field, checkpoint skip logic skips MIGRATED records on re-run, dry_run never calls Ironclad write endpoint. VCR cassettes must use filter_sensitive_data for all Salesforce IDs and Ironclad workflow IDs in recorded responses.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Salesforce CPQ orgs with heavy customisation may have non-standard amendment relationship fields not captured in default SOQL query; mitigated by exposing configurable SOQL template in Config and documenting common CPQ customisation patterns in gem README. Risk: Ironclad API version changes silently drop mapped fields; mitigated by pinning Accept-Version header to v2025-01 and logging a warning when API returns 200 with fewer fields than submitted. Tradeoff: BFS over DFS for traversal uses O(n) memory for the queue where n is amendment chain size; acceptable since chains rarely exceed 20 elements and memory is not the constraint. Tradeoff: SHA256 checkpoint keying adds a SHA256 computation per contract versus raw ID storage; negligible cost for the security benefit of not exposing Salesforce internal object IDs.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Amendment chain depth of 8+ confirmed supported with BFS implementation and max_amendment_depth=10 default with config override. LEDES XML not applicable to this gem (Ruby gem is for contract object metadata not billing). Ironclad API version pinned to v2025-01 in Accept-Version header with upgrade path documented.

metadata.notes: Ruby gem for Salesforce CPQ to Ironclad CLM contract migration; round 2 resolves amendment traversal cycle detection with BFS and visited set.

profile.domain: Legal Operations Platform Marketing

project_goal.value: Marketing landing page for a modular legal operations platform selling contract analysis, policy management, and litigation tracking microservices as standalone or composable products to enterprise legal departments
project_goal.rationale: Core positioning message emphasising a-la-carte microservice adoption for enterprise buyers

target_users.value: Enterprise legal operations buyers, GCs, and legal technology procurement leads at mid-to-large enterprises
target_users.rationale: Primary decision-makers evaluating modular legal ops tooling

project_class.rationale: Static marketing site with minimal dynamic behaviour; no server-side application runtime required

capabilities.rationale: Conversion-focused static page with analytics event tracking only

complexity_level.rationale: Static content with form integration; no database or auth requirements

risk_level.rationale: No user data stored; lead submissions routed to external CRM

data_sensitivity.rationale: No PII persisted on the site; Salesforce handles CRM data

external_exposure.rationale: Public marketing page with no authentication gate

access_model.value: Fully public; no authentication; GDPR cookie consent banner required for EU visitors
access_model.rationale: Open public site; GDPR compliance for EU visitors via Cookiebot

feature_scope.value: Module feature cards for contract analysis, policy management, and litigation tracking; pricing tier table; enterprise qualification form; case study carousel; microservices architecture explainer section
feature_scope.rationale: All sections needed to convey modular value proposition to enterprise buyers

mvp_scope.value: Hero section with CTA, three module feature cards, enterprise inquiry form routing to Salesforce, and Segment analytics integration
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed for initial campaign launch

security_baseline.value: HTTPS via Vercel TLS, GDPR-compliant cookie consent via Cookiebot, CSP headers, no server-side secrets in static bundle
security_baseline.rationale: Standard static site security posture

frontend_stack.value: Next.js 14 with TypeScript deployed as static export to Vercel Edge
frontend_stack.rationale: Next.js static export provides ISR for case study content updates without full rebuild

backend_stack.value: None; Vercel serverless function for Salesforce form submission only
backend_stack.rationale: No persistent backend required for a static marketing page

data_platform.value: None; no database; lead data lives in Salesforce CRM
data_platform.rationale: External CRM is the system of record for all lead submissions

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with global CDN
hosting_target.rationale: Zero-config Next.js hosting with sub-50ms TTFB globally

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained on-site; Salesforce GDPR data retention governs CRM records; cookie consent managed via Cookiebot
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: All retention obligations delegated to Salesforce and Cookiebot as data processors

future_scope.value: Localised pages for EMEA and APAC markets; product demo sandbox embedded in page; customer login portal link
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 internationalisation and conversion optimisation roadmap

constraints.value: Page must score 95+ on Lighthouse performance; all content managed via Contentful CMS without engineer involvement; launch in 3 weeks
constraints.rationale: Marketing team autonomy and aggressive launch timeline

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for Core Web Vitals, Segment for behavioural events, error alerting via Vercel deployment health checks
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability appropriate for static site

execution_preference.value: Single sprint delivery with Contentful CMS setup in week 1 and full page launch by end of week 3
execution_preference.rationale: Fast time-to-market for planned campaign

project.goal: Convert enterprise legal ops buyers by showcasing modular compliance microservices
project.target_users: GCs and legal technology procurement leads
project.timeline: 3-week launch

frontend.cms: Contentful for case studies and module descriptions

security.headers: CSP allowlisting Segment, Cookiebot, Salesforce domains

devops.ci: GitHub Actions running Lighthouse CI and TypeScript checks on PR

reasoner_reviews.product: The modular microservices positioning is a genuine differentiator for enterprise legal ops buyers who resist monolithic suite commitments. The three-module card layout maps directly to the contract analysis, policy management, and litigation tracking product lines. A multi-step qualification form capturing which modules the buyer needs will improve Salesforce lead scoring significantly over a generic contact form.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Next.js 14 static export to Vercel Edge is the correct choice — ISR on case study pages means marketing can update customer testimonials via Contentful without triggering a full rebuild. The Vercel serverless function for Salesforce form submission keeps the architecture dependency-light while avoiding client-side API key exposure in the static bundle.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP headers must explicitly allowlist Segment (cdn.segment.com), Cookiebot (consent.cookiebot.com), and Salesforce (login.salesforce.com) to prevent third-party script injection. The Salesforce OAuth2 client secret must be stored as a Vercel environment variable, never embedded in the static bundle.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 3-week timeline is achievable with the static export approach. Contentful space setup and content modelling for case studies should be prioritised in week 1 so marketing can populate content while engineering builds page components in parallel.

frontend.app_structure: Next.js 14 app router with static generation; pages: / (full landing), /modules/[slug] (individual module detail pages); Contentful SDK for build-time data fetching with ISR revalidation via webhook
frontend.pages_and_flows: Hero with dual CTA (start trial / book demo), three ModuleCard components (contract analysis, policy management, litigation tracking), MicroservicesArchitectureDiagram SVG explainer, PricingTierTable, CaseStudyCarousel from Contentful, EnterpriseQualificationForm with three-step routing to Salesforce
frontend.performance_notes: next/image with WebP for all module screenshots; critical CSS inlined; Tailwind purge reduces bundle size; font-display swap for heading fonts; Lighthouse CI gate at 95 mobile
frontend.frontend_risks: Contentful CMS schema changes post-launch may break ISR cache; mitigation: webhook-triggered revalidation with fallback to stale content rather than 404

security.auth_design: No authentication; fully public site
security.secrets_management: Salesforce Connected App client secret stored as Vercel environment variable SALESFORCE_CLIENT_SECRET; never included in static bundle output
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel serverless form endpoint rate-limited to 20 submissions per IP per hour via Vercel KV counter; honeypot field on EnterpriseQualificationForm to block automated bots

data.entities: No persistent entities; lead submissions are Salesforce Lead records; Segment event schema: module_card_clicked {module_name STRING, expanded BOOL}, qualification_form_step_completed {step INT, selected_modules ARRAY}, demo_booking_clicked {source ENUM hero/pricing}
data.analytics_events: Segment track calls on: hero CTA click (trial vs demo), module card expansion, pricing tier hover dwell >3s, qualification form step completions, case study carousel slide interactions
data.data_risks: Segment data residency defaults to US; EU visitor behavioural events subject to GDPR Article 6 lawful basis; Cookiebot must gate Segment script initialisation until consent granted

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel Edge with automatic preview deployments on every PR; production deployment triggered by merge to main branch
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on every PR: TypeScript typecheck, ESLint, Lighthouse CI (mobile threshold 95, accessibility 90); Contentful webhook triggers ISR revalidation on content publish
devops.environments: preview (per-PR Vercel branch URL with unique domain), production (legal-ops-platform.com via Vercel DNS)
devops.devops_risks: Contentful outage during ISR revalidation serves stale case study content; mitigation: fallback:true on getStaticPaths for module detail pages

backend.service_design: Single Vercel serverless function /api/submit-inquiry: authenticates to Salesforce via OAuth2 client credentials, maps form payload to Salesforce Lead fields (Company, selected_modules__c, company_size__c), returns 200 on success
backend.failure_handling: On Salesforce API timeout the function returns 503 and the client displays a retry prompt; failed submissions logged to Vercel function logs for manual follow-up
backend.backend_risks: Salesforce API rate limits on Developer edition must be upgraded to Enterprise tier before campaign launch to handle burst traffic

target_output.thinking_summary: Static Next.js 14 landing page on Vercel Edge is the right choice for a marketing site requiring 95+ Lighthouse scores and non-engineer content management. ISR with Contentful webhooks lets marketing update case studies without engineering involvement. The only server-side concern is the Salesforce lead submission endpoint, handled by a single Vercel serverless function with OAuth2 credentials stored as environment secrets — no secrets leak into the static bundle.
target_output.title: Modular Legal Operations Platform Landing Page Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static site deployed to Vercel Edge CDN, serving enterprise legal ops buyers with module-specific feature cards for contract analysis, policy management, and litigation tracking microservices. Contentful CMS drives case study and module content via ISR. A Vercel serverless function handles Salesforce Lead creation from multi-step enterprise qualification form submissions. Segment tracks module engagement events gated behind Cookiebot GDPR consent.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Next.js 14 site exported to Vercel Edge CDN with no persistent backend runtime. Pages are statically generated at build time with ISR for Contentful-managed content — case studies and module descriptions — revalidated via Contentful publish webhooks hitting /api/revalidate. The single dynamic surface is a Vercel serverless function at /api/submit-inquiry that authenticates to Salesforce via OAuth2 client credentials flow and creates Lead records from EnterpriseQualificationForm submissions. Segment analytics load conditionally after Cookiebot GDPR consent, ensuring no behavioural tracking before explicit opt-in from EU visitors.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 app router with TypeScript static export, Tailwind CSS, next/image WebP optimisation. CMS: Contentful with contentful TypeScript SDK for ISR data fetching. Analytics: Segment (behavioural events) + Vercel Analytics (Core Web Vitals). Form backend: Vercel serverless function calling Salesforce REST API via OAuth2 client credentials. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI mobile threshold 95. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with global CDN and automatic HTTPS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero section with dual CTA routing to free trial signup and demo booking flows. ModuleCard trio: contract analysis (AI clause extraction, risk flagging), policy management (version-controlled policy library, approval workflows), litigation tracking (docket sync, deadline monitoring). MicroservicesArchitectureDiagram SVG showing how modules compose via a shared API gateway layer. PricingTierTable with per-module and bundle pricing and a la carte tier callout. CaseStudyCarousel sourced from Contentful featuring named enterprise clients with measurable outcomes. EnterpriseQualificationForm capturing company size, modules of interest, and implementation timeline.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge CDN distributing statically generated HTML/CSS/JS bundles globally. Next.js ISR cache with Contentful webhook revalidation for /modules/[slug] and case study paths. Vercel serverless function /api/submit-inquiry for Salesforce Lead creation via OAuth2. Vercel serverless function /api/revalidate for Contentful webhook ISR trigger. Segment analytics script consent-gated by Cookiebot banner. GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse CI, TypeScript, and ESLint checks on every PR.
target_output.workflows: Enterprise Buyer Qualification: Visitor expands contract-analysis ModuleCard triggering module_card_clicked Segment event. Visitor clicks primary CTA and begins three-step EnterpriseQualificationForm: step 1 selects desired modules, step 2 provides company size and industry vertical, step 3 captures name, email, and timeline. Submission POST to /api/submit-inquiry; function exchanges OAuth2 credentials for Salesforce token (cached in function memory for token TTL), maps form data to Lead with selected_modules__c and company_size__c custom fields, returns 200. Salesforce triggers personalised demo booking email sequence. Segment fires qualification_form_step_completed on each step and qualification_form_completed on success. CMS Content Update: Marketing author publishes updated case study in Contentful. Contentful webhook fires POST to /api/revalidate with WEBHOOK_SECRET. Next.js on-demand ISR revalidates affected /case-studies/[slug] paths at next request without full rebuild.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model on site. Salesforce Lead record fields populated per submission: FirstName, LastName, Email, Company, selected_modules__c (multi-select picklist: contract_analysis/policy_management/litigation_tracking), company_size__c (picklist: 1-50/51-500/501-5000/5000+), implementation_timeline__c, LeadSource='landing_page_enterprise'. Segment event schema: module_card_clicked {module_name: 'contract_analysis'|'policy_management'|'litigation_tracking', expanded: bool}, qualification_form_step_completed {step: 1|2|3, selected_modules: string[]}, demo_booking_clicked {source: 'hero'|'pricing_table'|'module_card'}.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/submit-inquiry: accepts {first_name, last_name, email, company, company_size, selected_modules[], timeline, honeypot_field}; validates honeypot empty; applies Vercel KV rate limit 20/IP/hour; authenticates to Salesforce POST /services/oauth2/token with client credentials; creates Lead via POST /services/data/v60.0/sobjects/Lead; returns 200 {success: true} or 503 {error: 'submission_failed', retry: true}. GET /api/revalidate?secret=WEBHOOK_SECRET: verifies secret matches REVALIDATION_SECRET env var; calls res.revalidate() for affected Contentful page paths; returns 200 {revalidated: true}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced via Vercel TLS with HSTS preload header. Content Security Policy in next.config.js headers() explicitly allowlisting cdn.segment.com, consent.cookiebot.com, login.salesforce.com, and va.vercel-scripts.com; all other external scripts blocked. Salesforce OAuth2 client secret stored exclusively in SALESFORCE_CLIENT_SECRET Vercel environment variable; never present in static bundle. Honeypot field and Vercel KV rate limit (20 submissions per IP per hour) protect form endpoint. Cookiebot GDPR consent banner gates Segment script injection — EU visitors receive no behavioural analytics until opt-in confirmed. REVALIDATION_SECRET environment variable guards the ISR revalidation endpoint from unauthorised cache invalidation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR: bun run typecheck, bun run lint, and Lighthouse CI with mobile performance >=95 and accessibility >=90 thresholds; PRs failing thresholds are blocked from merge. Vercel creates a unique preview deployment URL per PR, posted as a PR comment for review. Production deployment triggers automatically on merge to main; Vercel builds Next.js static export and distributes to Edge within 30 seconds. Contentful content publish triggers /api/revalidate webhook to refresh ISR cache for affected pages without a rebuild cycle. Rollback via Vercel dashboard instant rollback to prior deployment — no downtime required.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics tracking Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) per page with mobile/desktop breakdowns. Segment workspace feeding a Looker Studio conversion dashboard: module_card_clicked breakdown by module_name, qualification_form funnel from step 1 to submission, demo_booking_clicked conversion rate by source. Vercel deployment failure alerts posted to #marketing-eng Slack channel via Vercel Slack integration. Lighthouse CI trend tracked in GitHub Actions PR comments to catch performance regressions early.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan $20/month covers Edge CDN, serverless function invocations, and preview deployments. Contentful Team plan $300/month for multi-author CMS with content scheduling. Salesforce Enterprise tier for API quota. Total approximately $350/month. Vercel Edge auto-scales to any traffic volume without capacity planning — static site architecture eliminates server scaling concerns entirely.
target_output.phased_implementation: Week 1: Contentful space setup with Module, CaseStudy, and PricingTier content models; Vercel project with environment variables and DNS configuration; Next.js app scaffold with Tailwind, Contentful SDK, and ISR configuration. Marketing team populates initial content in parallel. Week 2: Hero section, ModuleCard trio, PricingTierTable, and CaseStudyCarousel component builds; Segment event instrumentation; /api/submit-inquiry serverless function with Salesforce OAuth2 integration; Cookiebot GDPR banner configuration. Week 3: EnterpriseQualificationForm three-step flow; Lighthouse CI gate enforcement in GitHub Actions; accessibility audit against WCAG 2.1 AA; QA on preview deployment across mobile and desktop; production DNS cutover and launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode throughout; no any escape hatches without justification comment. Contentful content types have generated TypeScript interfaces via contentful-typescript-codegen — never use raw any for CMS responses. All Segment track() calls go through a typed analytics.ts wrapper module; no direct window.analytics calls scattered through components. Lighthouse CI thresholds enforced in GitHub Actions; regressions block PR merge. Salesforce API credentials consumed only in Vercel serverless functions; handlers must never import SALESFORCE_CLIENT_SECRET in client components.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Contentful outage during ISR revalidation results in stale case study content served to visitors. Mitigation: Next.js fallback:true on module detail pages serves stale content rather than 404; Contentful 99.9% SLA is acceptable. Risk: Salesforce OAuth2 token refresh adds 200-400ms latency to first form submission after function cold start. Mitigation: token cached in function memory for its TTL (~1 hour) so subsequent submissions within the same warm instance are unaffected. Tradeoff: ISR over full static generation means build times stay fast as the case study library grows to 50+ entries; the tradeoff is webhook infrastructure dependency for cache freshness. Tradeoff: Segment over direct GA4 enables CRM-level event enrichment and cross-platform identity resolution at the cost of an additional vendor and consent complexity.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose Contentful over Sanity for stronger TypeScript SDK and enterprise support tier availability. Chose Salesforce Lead over Opportunity object for top-of-funnel submissions — sales team converts manually. Chose Cookiebot over custom consent implementation for pre-built IAB TCF 2.2 compliance and CNIL certification. Lighthouse threshold set at 95 not 100 to accommodate Cookiebot and Segment scripts without prohibitive bundle optimisation effort.

metadata.notes: Static Next.js marketing site for modular legal ops platform; Contentful ISR with Salesforce lead routing; Lighthouse 95 CI gate; GDPR Cookiebot consent.

profile.domain: Infrastructure Compliance Scanning CLI

project_goal.value: CLI tool for compliance engineers to scan Terraform and CloudFormation IaC repositories against CIS Benchmarks, NIST 800-53, and PCI-DSS control rules, producing SARIF findings reports and blocking non-compliant configurations in CI pipelines
project_goal.rationale: Automates infrastructure compliance checks that currently require manual review against control libraries

target_users.value: Compliance engineers and platform teams running IaC security reviews and enforcing policy gates in CI pipelines
target_users.rationale: Primary operators are security-focused infrastructure engineers

project_class.rationale: Command-line binary distributed via GitHub Releases; no web interface or hosted backend

capabilities.rationale: File parsing, rule evaluation engine, and output format integration; no frontend, auth, or hosting required

complexity_level.rationale: HCL/JSON AST parsing and rule evaluation are non-trivial but bounded in scope for a CLI tool

risk_level.rationale: Tool processes potentially sensitive IaC configurations; findings output must not leak secrets from variable files

data_sensitivity.rationale: IaC templates contain internal infrastructure topology; no user PII or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: CLI binary runs locally or in CI agents; no network exposure

access_model.rationale: CLI tool with local file access only; no user accounts or API keys

mvp_scope.rationale: Most common use case for initial CI pipeline integration

security_baseline.value: No network calls during scan; findings output must redact Terraform variable values that may contain secrets; binary signed with cosign for supply chain verification
security_baseline.rationale: Tool processes IaC that may reference secrets; no exfiltration risk during offline scan

frontend_stack.value: None - CLI binary only; output to stdout/stderr and optional output file
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI tool with no web frontend

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with standard library; hashicorp/hcl v2 for HCL parsing; go-yaml for policy definitions
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces fast single-binary CLI tools; hashicorp/hcl is the canonical Terraform HCL parser

data_platform.value: None - stateless CLI reading local files; policy packs distributed as embedded Go assets via go:embed
data_platform.rationale: No persistent storage required for a stateless scan tool

hosting_target.value: GitHub Releases binary distribution (linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64); also published as container image to GHCR for CI use
hosting_target.rationale: Native binary for direct CLI use; container image for CI agent integration without local install

privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Stateless CLI with no server-side data storage

constraints.value: Must run offline with no network calls during scan; must complete scan of 1,000 Terraform files in under 30 seconds on standard CI agent hardware; binary size under 50MB
constraints.rationale: CI agents may have restricted network access; scan speed affects CI pipeline duration

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON scan summary written to stdout; verbose mode with per-rule timing via --verbose flag; no server-side telemetry
observability_baseline.rationale: CLI tool observability is local scan output only; no external monitoring infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Ship iteratively: Terraform HCL parser first, then CloudFormation, then custom YAML rule authoring
execution_preference.rationale: Terraform is the dominant IaC format in target user environments

project.goal: Offline CLI scanner for Terraform HCL and CloudFormation against CIS, NIST, PCI-DSS policy packs
project.target_users: Compliance engineers and platform teams in CI pipelines
project.timeline: Round 2 addressing suppression validation and SARIF schema gaps

security.secrets_redaction: Terraform sensitive variable values masked in output

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with go test, go vet, golangci-lint

reasoner_reviews.product: The suppression bypass (ISS-001) is a security control integrity issue: if engineers can suppress non-existent rule IDs, adding a new rule later may be silently bypassed in codebases with pre-existing suppressions. The SARIF gap (ISS-002) blocks the primary CI integration use case with GitHub Advanced Security. Both are blocking for production adoption.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ISS-001 fix is straightforward: build a rule_id set from the loaded policy pack manifest at scan initialization and validate each suppression against it. ISS-002 requires replacing the hand-rolled SARIF map with typed Go structs that enforce required fields at compile time. Using GitHub's SARIF schema test fixtures as acceptance criteria is the right approach.
reasoner_reviews.security: The suppression validation fix should also support a --strict-suppressions flag that exits non-zero on any suppression referencing a non-existent rule ID, allowing security teams to treat stale suppressions as policy violations in high-assurance environments.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Both fixes are localized to the suppression validator and SARIF output modules respectively; they should not affect scan performance. The SARIF struct refactor must keep the existing JSON output path unchanged.

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with cmd/ package containing scan, policy, config subcommands; internal/parser package for HCL and CloudFormation parsers; internal/engine package for rule evaluation; internal/output package for SARIF, JSON, and table formatters; internal/policy package for policy pack loading and suppression validation
backend.api_patterns: CLI flags: iacscanner scan --path ./terraform --policy-pack cis-aws-1.4 --output sarif --output-file findings.sarif --fail-on high; suppression via inline HCL comment: # iacscanner:suppress RULE-ID justification='...' ticket='JIRA-123'
backend.failure_handling: Non-zero exit code (1) on findings at or above --fail-on severity threshold; exit code 2 for scan errors (unparseable files); exit code 0 for clean scan. Per-file parse errors written to stderr with filename and line number; scan continues on non-fatal parse errors
backend.scaling_notes: Parallel file parsing using Go goroutines with worker pool sized to runtime.NumCPU(); 1,000-file scan target <30 seconds on 4-core CI agent

security.auth_design: No network calls during scan; no authentication required. Binary signed with cosign using Sigstore for supply chain verification; GitHub Actions release workflow signs binary and uploads SBOM
security.secrets_management: Findings output redacts Terraform variable values with sensitive=true annotation; variable default values masked as REDACTED in SARIF physicalLocation excerpts
security.abuse_prevention: Path traversal protection: --path argument validated against allowed directory list; symlink following disabled by default; --allow-symlinks flag required for symlink traversal

data.entities: iac_resources (resource_type, resource_name, file_path, start_line, attributes_map), control_rules (rule_id, policy_pack, severity, control_framework, description, check_function), findings (rule_id, resource_type, resource_name, file_path, start_line, severity, message, suppressed), policy_packs (name, version, framework, rules[]), suppression_entries (rule_id, resource_address, justification, ticket, annotated_at)
data.storage_design: All data in-memory during scan; policy packs embedded via go:embed as YAML assets compiled into binary; no disk writes except optional --output-file
data.migration_notes: Policy pack versioning via semver in pack manifest; iacscanner policy list shows installed packs; custom YAML rules merged with built-in packs at scan time

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions release workflow: build cross-platform binaries (linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64) via GOOS/GOARCH matrix; cosign sign with ephemeral GitHub OIDC key; SBOM generation via syft; Docker multi-arch image pushed to GHCR
devops.ci_cd_design: PR CI: go test ./..., go vet ./..., golangci-lint, race detector tests. Release CI: goreleaser for cross-compile and archive packaging; GitHub Release with checksums file
devops.observability_stack: No server-side monitoring; CLI --verbose flag emits per-rule evaluation timing to stderr; --debug flag logs AST node counts and rule match attempts

ISS-001.title: Suppression validation does not verify rule ID against loaded policy pack
ISS-001.detail: In round 1, suppression annotations are accepted with any rule_id string without checking that the rule exists in the loaded policy pack, allowing engineers to suppress non-existent rules and silently bypass future rule additions under the same ID

history.note: Suppression validation logic does not cross-reference rule IDs against policy pack manifest

ISS-002.title: SARIF output schema not validated against SARIF 2.1.0 spec before write
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 SARIF output generation is hand-rolled without schema validation; GitHub Advanced Security SARIF ingestion has failed in integration testing due to missing required fields (runs[].tool.driver.informationUri and results[].locations[].physicalLocation.region.startLine)

history.note: SARIF output missing required fields for GitHub Advanced Security ingestion

focus_issues.detail: Suppression annotations accepted with any rule_id string without verifying rule exists in policy pack

latest_recommendations: Validate suppression rule IDs against the loaded policy pack manifest at scan start; emit a warning for each suppression referencing a non-existent rule ID
Implement SARIF 2.1.0 struct types in Go with required fields and validate output against the schema before writing; use GitHub's SARIF SDK test fixtures as acceptance criteria

best_plan.architecture_overview: Single Go binary with three command groups: scan (parse + evaluate + report), policy (list/validate policy packs), and config (generate suppression annotations). Policy packs embedded as YAML via go:embed. HCL parsing via hashicorp/hcl v2 producing resource AST. Rule evaluation engine iterates resource nodes against loaded policy pack rules.
