previous_plan.title: AI Legal Research Assistant Architecture - Round 3
previous_plan.executive_summary: RAG-based legal research assistant with hybrid pgvector plus BM25 retrieval, LangChain RetrievalQA with Claude 3 Sonnet via Bedrock, and Okta SSO. Round 3 resolved admin privilege visibility and improved retrieval quality but deferred sentence-level citation spans.
previous_plan.data_model: document_chunks (id, document_id, chunk_text, embedding vector(1536), tsvector_content, token_count). query_sessions (id, user_id, question_text, answer_text, retrieved_chunk_ids UUID[], answer_trace_json JSONB storing chunk IDs only without span positions).
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: PostgreSQL RLS on query_sessions. Admin analytics restricted to usage_summaries aggregate table. Bedrock no-logging option enabled.
previous_plan.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate for FastAPI, Celery workers for ingestion. Full HNSW index rebuild triggered by corpus admin on each new batch upload.

best_plan.title: AI Legal Research Assistant Architecture - Round 3
best_plan.executive_summary: Best scoring plan to date (7.8). Strong hybrid retrieval and security posture. Two issues blocking convergence: citation spans and incremental ingestion.
best_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI RAG pipeline with LangChain, pgvector hybrid search, Redis embedding cache. Next.js 14 streaming UI. Okta SSO. RDS PostgreSQL 16 us-east-1.
best_plan.security_and_compliance: RLS on query_sessions, Bedrock no-logging, admin aggregate-only analytics, us-east-1 data residency.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 4 addressing two remaining issues from round 3: (1) sentence-level citation span tracking for attorney reliance safety and (2) incremental HNSW index update strategy to prevent corpus addition latency spikes. For ISS-001: CitationBuilder extended to run sentence boundary detection (spaCy sentencizer) on each retrieved chunk, extract candidate supporting sentences, and annotate answer_trace_json with span_start_char and span_end_char per citation. UI updated to highlight the specific sentence within the chunk in the citation side panel. For ISS-003: switched from triggering a full HNSW rebuild to using a pg_partman partition-per-ingestion-batch approach with a union-all view, periodically merged into the main index during low-traffic windows via a maintenance job. These are the final two blocking issues; round 4 should achieve convergence if both are fully addressed.

fix_report.action_taken: Extended CitationBuilder with spaCy sentencizer to detect sentence boundaries in each retrieved chunk; for each claim sentence in the synthesized answer, CitationBuilder identifies the best-matching chunk sentence using cosine similarity of sentence embeddings and records span_start_char and span_end_char in answer_trace_json; updated document_chunks schema to store pre-computed sentence boundary offsets; updated citation side panel in Next.js UI to highlight the specific supporting sentence within the chunk text
fix_report.expected_outcome: Attorneys can see the exact sentence in each retrieved chunk that supports a specific claim in the answer, enabling reliable citation verification before court filing

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced full HNSW rebuild on corpus addition with a partition-per-batch ingestion strategy: new document chunks inserted into a daily partition table (document_chunks_YYYY_MM_DD), searched via UNION ALL view combining all partitions for queries; nightly maintenance job (2am ET) runs CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on any new partitions and merges small partitions into the main index via pg_partman when total partition count exceeds 7 days
fix_report.expected_outcome: New document additions no longer trigger disruptive HNSW rebuilds; query latency remains under 150ms p95 during corpus updates; maintenance window merges handled without blocking query path

target_output.title: AI Legal Research Assistant Architecture - Round 4
target_output.executive_summary: A RAG-based AI legal research assistant for internal law firm use, providing attorneys with natural-language research queries answered with sentence-level cited synthesis from a curated corpus of case opinions, statutes, and regulations. Built on FastAPI with LangChain, hybrid pgvector plus BM25 retrieval, and Claude 3 Sonnet via AWS Bedrock in us-east-1. Round 4 completes the architecture by implementing sentence-level citation span tracking in the CitationBuilder and answer_traces schema (resolving attorney reliance risk) and a partition-per-batch HNSW index strategy for incremental corpus additions without query path disruption.
target_output.architecture_overview: Next.js 14 App Router frontend streams LLM token output via server-sent events from a FastAPI async endpoint. The RAG pipeline operates as: query embedding via Bedrock Titan (Redis-cached 24h) then HybridRetriever fetches top-8 chunks via pgvector ANN search (HNSW index ef_search=100) fused with BM25 tsvector ranking via RRF; retrieved chunks fed to LangChain RetrievalQA with Claude 3 Sonnet (temperature 0, corpus-only citation instruction); CitationBuilder uses spaCy sentencizer to annotate each claim with the supporting sentence span_start_char and span_end_char from the source chunk; answer_trace persisted to RDS PostgreSQL with full citation provenance. Document corpus stored as partitioned document_chunks tables with daily partition creation for new ingestion batches; queries run against a UNION ALL view; nightly maintenance merges partitions via CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY. All data in us-east-1, Bedrock no-logging enabled, RLS on query_sessions, Okta SAML SSO with MFA.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 App Router, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, TipTap for citation annotation rendering, Zustand for streaming state. API: Python 3.12, FastAPI, LangChain 0.2 (RetrievalQA chain, BedrockChat), spaCy 3.7 (sentencizer for citation span detection). LLM: AWS Bedrock Claude 3 Sonnet (inference), Bedrock Titan Embeddings V2 (1536-dim). Database: RDS PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector 0.7 (HNSW index), pg_partman for partition management, RLS. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 for embedding cache and rate limiting. Storage: S3 us-east-1 for raw legal documents (SSE-S3). Auth: Okta SAML 2.0, JWT RS256. Infra: ECS Fargate, GitHub Actions, AWS Amplify, Datadog LLM Observability.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Research Query Interface: streaming answer display with inline citation markers [1][2]; citation markers interactive - clicking opens side panel with source chunk showing highlighted supporting sentence identified by CitationBuilder span. Session History: paginated list of past research queries with question excerpt, answer quality indicator (attorney feedback star rating), and date. Citation Side Panel: full chunk text with the specific supporting sentence highlighted in amber; link to full source document (S3 presigned URL 15-min TTL); copy citation button formatting as Bluebook citation. Feedback System: 1-5 star rating and optional note on each answer; stored in query_sessions; negative ratings flagged for corpus quality review. Admin Corpus Panel: document upload dropzone, ingestion queue status showing partition assignment and chunk count, partition merge schedule. Admin Analytics: monthly token usage and cost by practice group from usage_summaries aggregate table only.
target_output.system_components: HybridRetriever: pgvector cosine ANN search (HNSW, ef_search=100) combined with PostgreSQL tsvector BM25 via Reciprocal Rank Fusion; queries document_chunks UNION ALL view spanning all active partitions. CitationBuilder: post-answer-generation component using spaCy sentencizer to split each retrieved chunk into sentence segments; for each citation in the synthesized answer, identifies best-matching sentence via cosine similarity of sentence embeddings; records span_start_char and span_end_char in answer_trace_json citation array. Corpus IngestionPipeline: Celery task triggered by S3 upload event; splits PDF/text into 512-token chunks with 50-token overlap; embeds via Bedrock Titan; inserts into daily partition document_chunks_{date}; updates document_chunks_view to include new partition. Partition Maintenance Job: nightly ECS Scheduled Task at 2am ET; runs pg_partman merge for partitions older than 7 days into main document_chunks table with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY; no query blocking. AnswerTraceWriter: persists full answer with answer_trace_json including citation array (chunk_id, document_id, span_start_char, span_end_char, supporting_sentence_text) to query_sessions. LLMSynthesizer: LangChain RetrievalQA with BedrockChat, max_tokens 2048, temperature 0, system prompt with corpus-only citation constraint.
target_output.workflows: Research Query Workflow: attorney submits natural-language question via Next.js query interface; FastAPI ResearchRouter receives query; embedding computed (Redis cache check then Bedrock Titan if miss); HybridRetriever fetches top-8 chunks from UNION ALL view via pgvector ANN plus BM25 RRF fusion; chunks plus question sent to LangChain RetrievalQA with Claude 3 Sonnet via Bedrock streaming; tokens streamed back via server-sent events to Next.js; on answer completion CitationBuilder runs spaCy sentencizer on each retrieved chunk, matches claims to supporting sentences, annotates answer_trace_json with span positions; AnswerTraceWriter persists full trace to query_sessions; attorney clicks citation marker to see highlighted sentence in side panel. Corpus Ingestion Workflow (incremental): corpus admin uploads legal document PDF via admin panel; S3 upload event triggers Celery ingestion task; PDF split into 512-token chunks; Bedrock Titan embeddings computed; chunks inserted into daily partition document_chunks_{today}; partition registered in document_chunks_view; ingestion complete with no HNSW rebuild or query disruption; nightly maintenance job merges partitions older than 7 days via pg_partman with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
target_output.data_model: legal_documents (id UUID PK, title, citation_reference, document_type ENUM case_opinion/statute/regulation, jurisdiction, publication_date, s3_key, ingested_at, chunk_count INT). document_chunks (id UUID PK, document_id FK, chunk_index INT, chunk_text TEXT, embedding vector(1536), tsvector_content tsvector, token_count INT, sentence_boundaries JSONB storing array of {start_char, end_char, sentence_text} computed at ingestion by spaCy sentencizer, created_at) - partitioned by ingestion_date with pg_partman; queries via document_chunks_view UNION ALL. query_sessions (id UUID PK, user_id FK, question_text TEXT, answer_text TEXT, retrieved_chunk_ids UUID[], answer_trace_json JSONB: {citations: [{chunk_id, document_id, span_start_char, span_end_char, supporting_sentence_text, citation_index}]}, feedback_rating INT NULLABLE, feedback_note TEXT NULLABLE, created_at, latency_ms) with RLS WHERE user_id = current_setting. usage_summaries (id, period_start, period_end, practice_group, total_queries, total_tokens, estimated_cost_usd).
target_output.api_design: POST /research/query (body: {question: string max 500 chars, session_id?: UUID}; streams server-sent events: data: {type: token, content: string} and data: {type: citation, index: int, chunk_id: UUID, span_start_char: int, span_end_char: int, supporting_sentence: string} and data: {type: done, session_id: UUID, latency_ms: int}). GET /sessions (returns paginated array of {id, question_excerpt, feedback_rating, created_at}; RLS enforced). GET /sessions/:id/trace (returns full answer_trace_json with citation spans; RLS enforced). GET /chunks/:id/highlight?span_start=N&span_end=M (returns chunk_text with HTML span tags wrapping the cited sentence for UI rendering). POST /corpus/documents (admin only: multipart PDF upload, returns ingestion job_id). GET /corpus/partitions (admin only: list of active partitions with chunk count and merge status). GET /analytics/usage?period=2026-Q1 (admin only: aggregated from usage_summaries).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta SAML 2.0 with MFA for all users; FastAPI JWT validation via python-jose with JWKS endpoint. RLS on query_sessions: SET LOCAL app.current_user_id = :user_id on connection checkout; policy WHERE user_id = current_setting enforces attorney-only access. Admin endpoints: JWT claim roles array must contain corpus_admin or system_admin. Bedrock API calls: no-logging option set in InferenceConfiguration per API call preventing Anthropic from training on firm queries. S3 source documents: private bucket, no public access, presigned GET URLs 15-minute TTL for citation click-through. Query text treated as privileged: never logged to Datadog or CloudWatch; only session_id and latency_ms in observability data. ABA Formal Opinion 512: supervisory notice displayed in UI below every answer - 'AI-synthesized answer requires attorney verification before reliance in legal filings.' answer_trace_json citation spans enable attorney verification of cited material.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate: FastAPI service (2-8 tasks, CPU 70% target auto-scaling), Celery ingestion worker (1-4 tasks, Redis queue depth auto-scaling), Partition Maintenance ECS Scheduled Task (nightly 2am ET). AWS Amplify hosts Next.js build with Okta callback URLs configured. GitHub Actions CI: pytest unit tests for HybridRetriever, CitationBuilder span extraction, and IngestionPipeline; Next.js type check and ESLint. Merge to main: Docker push to ECR, ECS rolling update, Amplify Next.js deploy. Pre-production gate: RAG quality benchmark with 50 legal research questions requiring recall@5 of 0.85+ and citation span accuracy of 80%+ (supporting sentence contains the cited claim). Environments: dev (single AZ, shared RDS dev schema), staging (multi-AZ, production data snapshot), production (Multi-AZ RDS us-east-1, ElastiCache cluster replica). Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for Bedrock API config, RDS credentials, Okta client secret.
target_output.observability: Datadog LLM Observability: token counts per query (input and output), TTFT histogram, citation verification check (validates cited chunk_id exists in retrieved_chunk_ids array), estimated per-query cost. Datadog APM with FastAPI tracer: full RAG pipeline span breakdown - embedding_ms, retrieval_ms, llm_ttft_ms, citation_build_ms, trace_write_ms. Custom metrics: retrieval_hit_rate (fraction of queries with 5+ high-similarity chunks), feedback_positive_rate (4-5 star / total), partition_merge_duration_ms (nightly maintenance), daily_bedrock_cost_usd. PagerDuty escalation: RAG pipeline error rate above 3% for 5 minutes, TTFT p95 above 5 seconds, daily Bedrock cost exceeding $150, partition maintenance job failure.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Bedrock Claude 3 Sonnet: approximately $0.003 per query at 1000 input tokens plus 500 output tokens. Redis embedding cache reduces Bedrock Titan embedding calls by approximately 30% for repeated legal terms. At 200 daily queries: approximately $18/day Bedrock cost. ECS Fargate auto-scales 2-8 tasks based on CPU target. Reserved Instances for RDS Multi-AZ baseline: $400/month. Total estimated monthly cost at 200 daily active queries: approximately $2800/month (RDS $400, Fargate $600, ElastiCache $150, Bedrock $540, Amplify $50, Datadog $500, S3 $60).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 - MVP (weeks 1-14): core RAG pipeline with hybrid retrieval, LangChain RetrievalQA with Claude 3 Sonnet via Bedrock, streaming Next.js UI with chunk-level citations, Okta SSO with MFA, PostgreSQL RLS on query_sessions, session history, basic corpus admin upload, RAG quality benchmark gate in CI. Phase 2 - Citation Quality and Incremental Ingestion (weeks 15-20): CitationBuilder sentence-level span tracking, updated answer_trace_json schema, highlighted citation side panel, partition-per-batch corpus ingestion with nightly merge maintenance job, attorney feedback star rating system. Phase 3 - Analytics and Compliance Hardening (weeks 21-24): admin usage analytics dashboard from usage_summaries, Datadog LLM Observability cost alerts, ABA Op 512 supervisory notice in UI, citation validity pre-check against Bluebook format, practice group usage reporting.
target_output.development_guidelines: CitationBuilder must have unit tests asserting span extraction accuracy on a fixture set of 20 retrieved chunks with known supporting sentences. HybridRetriever tested with mock pgvector results and BM25 scores to verify RRF fusion output ordering. No raw query text in any log statement - use session_id only. LLM synthesis tests use mocked Bedrock responses with pre-defined citation indices to test CitationBuilder span matching deterministically. RAG quality benchmark (50 legal questions gold standard) run in CI on staging environment as pre-production gate. Partition maintenance job has idempotency test: re-running on already-merged partitions must produce no change.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: spaCy sentencizer sentence boundary detection may fail on legal citation formats like 'Smith v. Jones, 500 F.3d 100 (2d Cir. 2007)' which contain periods that are not sentence terminators. Mitigation: custom spaCy sentencizer rules for legal citation patterns added to the pipeline before boundary detection. Risk: UNION ALL view over many small partitions degrades query performance as partition count grows. Mitigation: pg_partman merge keeps active partition count below 8 (7 daily plus main); Datadog alert if view query plan shows more than 10 partition scans. Tradeoff: sentence-level citation span detection adds approximately 80ms to answer generation pipeline (spaCy sentencizer plus embedding similarity matching); accepted given the attorney reliance safety benefit and the 2-second TTFT budget.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed spaCy sentencizer over NLTK sentence tokenizer for citation span detection due to better handling of legal text patterns and custom rule extensibility. Selected pg_partman partition merge strategy over a separate pgvector index per partition because partition merge keeps the query plan simple (single HNSW index post-merge) versus a multi-index union search. Decided sentence_boundaries JSONB pre-computed at ingestion time rather than at query time to avoid adding spaCy processing to the query latency critical path.

metadata.notes: Round 4 revision for AI legal research assistant. Resolves sentence-level citation span tracking via spaCy CitationBuilder and incremental HNSW corpus ingestion via pg_partman partition strategy. Two prior rounds of issues fully addressed.

profile.domain: Data Privacy - GDPR Article 30 RoPA Automation Prototype

project_goal.value: Research prototype for automated GDPR Article 30 Records of Processing Activities (RoPA) generation using LLM-assisted data flow discovery across enterprise system inventories, helping DPOs produce and maintain RoPA documentation without manual interviews across business units
project_goal.rationale: Manual RoPA interview cycles take 6-12 months and produce stale documentation within weeks; LLM-assisted discovery can reduce effort by 70% while improving coverage

target_users.value: Data Protection Officers and privacy compliance teams at enterprises with complex system inventories spanning 50+ applications
target_users.rationale: DPOs are the primary users who review, amend, and approve generated RoPA records

project_class.rationale: Research prototype validating LLM-assisted data flow inference accuracy before production investment

capabilities.rationale: FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL for RoPA records, LLM inference via Azure OpenAI, analytics on RoPA coverage gaps

complexity_level.rationale: LLM inference pipeline with structured output validation and DPO review workflow; moderate backend complexity

risk_level.rationale: RoPA accuracy directly affects regulatory compliance; incorrect processing activity records expose the organisation to GDPR enforcement risk

data_sensitivity.rationale: System inventory descriptions may contain personal data references; RoPA records describe personal data processing activities

external_exposure.rationale: DPO-only access via Azure AD authentication; no public endpoint

access_model.value: Azure AD OIDC authentication; DPO role required for all access; read-only analyst role for privacy team members who may view but not approve records
access_model.rationale: Only authorised DPO staff may approve RoPA records given regulatory accountability

feature_scope.value: System inventory upload (CSV or API import); LLM-assisted data flow inference producing draft processing_activity records; DPO review interface for approve/amend/reject; GDPR Article 30 field completeness validation; JSON-LD and PDF export of approved RoPA; coverage gap analytics dashboard; processing activity versioning
feature_scope.rationale: Full RoPA lifecycle from discovery to export

mvp_scope.value: System inventory CSV upload, LLM inference of draft RoPA records for 5-10 representative systems, DPO review and approval flow, JSON-LD export of approved records
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimal viable prototype to validate LLM inference accuracy with real DPO feedback before scaling

security_baseline.value: Azure AD OIDC for authentication, HTTPS TLS 1.3, PostgreSQL data encrypted at rest via Azure Disk Encryption, Azure OpenAI data processing agreement in place, audit log of all DPO review actions
security_baseline.rationale: High-risk processing requires strong authentication and audit trail given personal data in system inventory

frontend_stack.value: Streamlit for DPO review interface; no custom frontend build required for research prototype
frontend_stack.rationale: Streamlit enables rapid prototype UI iteration without frontend engineering overhead

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; Azure OpenAI GPT-4o for LLM inference with structured JSON output; LangChain for prompt orchestration and output parsing
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for async LLM inference endpoints; LangChain for structured output validation and retry on malformed LLM responses

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server for RoPA records and system inventory; no cache layer in prototype
data_platform.rationale: Relational model suits RoPA record relationships between processing activities, data categories, and legal bases

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Instances for prototype deployment; Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server; Azure OpenAI in same region
hosting_target.rationale: ACI provides simple container hosting without Kubernetes overhead for a prototype

privacy_retention_policy.value: System inventory data retained for prototype duration plus 90 days; RoPA records retained 3 years from approval for GDPR accountability obligations; audit logs retained 2 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 5(2) accountability principle requires DPO to demonstrate RoPA maintenance history

future_scope.value: Automated system inventory discovery via API connector library; continuous RoPA monitoring with change detection alerts; multi-jurisdiction RoPA templates (CCPA, LGPD); integration with OneTrust or TrustArc for production RoPA management
future_scope.rationale: Production system would replace prototype with automated connectors and enterprise DSAR integration

constraints.value: Azure OpenAI data must not be sent to non-Azure LLM providers; system inventory data must not leave the Azure tenant; prototype must run on a single Azure subscription under $500/month
constraints.rationale: Data residency and budget constraints for prototype phase

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor application logs; FastAPI request logging with DPO user identity; LLM inference latency and token usage metrics; RoPA record approval rate tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: Prototype observability focused on LLM accuracy and DPO workflow adoption metrics

execution_preference.value: Iterative prototype cycles with DPO user testing after each cycle; LLM prompt tuning based on DPO feedback on draft accuracy
execution_preference.rationale: Prototype must validate LLM inference accuracy with real DPO experts before architectural commitment

llm_integration.value: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with structured JSON output (response_format: json_schema); system prompt defining GDPR Article 30 RoPA field taxonomy; LangChain output parser with Pydantic validation; retry on schema validation failure up to 3 attempts
llm_integration.rationale: Structured output mode ensures LLM produces well-formed RoPA records; Pydantic validation catches field errors before DPO review

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 30 mandatory RoPA requirements for controllers processing personal data; EDPB guidelines on RoPA completeness; GDPR Article 35 DPIA trigger assessment based on high-risk processing activities identified in RoPA
compliance_context.rationale: RoPA is a mandatory compliance obligation under GDPR Article 30 for organisations with 250+ employees or high-risk processing

project.goal: Validate LLM-assisted GDPR Article 30 RoPA generation accuracy with DPO user testing
project.target_users: DPOs and privacy compliance teams
project.timeline: MVP prototype in 6 weeks; 3 user testing cycles

backend.framework: FastAPI with async LLM inference endpoints
backend.llm: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o structured output with LangChain
backend.validation: Pydantic models for RoPA record schema

security.auth: Azure AD OIDC; DPO and analyst roles
security.data: Azure Disk Encryption for PostgreSQL; no system inventory data sent outside Azure tenant

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: pytest, mypy, ruff linting

reasoner_reviews.product: The prototype correctly targets DPOs as the validation gatekeepers — their feedback on LLM inference accuracy is the primary success metric. The round 1 plan did not specify how the LLM handles ambiguous system descriptions where data subjects and legal bases cannot be confidently inferred; the prototype needs an explicit low-confidence flag that routes ambiguous records to a DPO clarification queue rather than presenting them as draft-ready suggestions.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI async endpoints with Azure OpenAI structured JSON output and LangChain retry on Pydantic validation failure is the correct architecture for reliable RoPA record generation. The round 1 plan lacked a prompt versioning strategy — prompt changes materially affect output quality and must be tracked alongside code changes to enable regression testing against a held-out set of system inventory fixtures.
reasoner_reviews.security: System inventory descriptions that include personal data references (e.g., 'HR system storing employee NI numbers') must not be sent to any LLM provider outside the Azure tenant. The round 1 plan confirmed Azure OpenAI usage but did not specify that the Azure OpenAI API call must use the managed identity credential rather than an API key stored as a secret, to avoid key rotation management overhead.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o token costs at prototype scale (100 system descriptions per run) are approximately $0.50 per full run — well within $500/month budget. The 3-attempt LangChain retry on Pydantic schema failure should have a dead-letter queue for persistently malformed outputs so DPOs can flag them as training signal for prompt improvement.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with routers: /api/inventory (CSV upload and system parsing), /api/infer (trigger LLM RoPA inference for a system), /api/records (CRUD on processing_activity records), /api/export (JSON-LD and PDF generation). Background task queue via FastAPI BackgroundTasks for LLM inference jobs to avoid HTTP timeout on long-running GPT-4o calls
backend.api_patterns: RESTful JSON API; LLM inference triggered asynchronously with job_id returned immediately; client polls /api/infer/{job_id}/status; Pydantic request/response models for all endpoints; Azure AD JWT validated on every request via python-jose
backend.business_modules: SystemInventoryParser: reads CSV columns (system_name, system_description, data_owner, business_unit, api_docs_url), validates and stores to system_inventory table. RoPAInferenceService: constructs GPT-4o prompt from system description and GDPR Article 30 field taxonomy, calls Azure OpenAI with response_format json_schema, validates output against ProcessingActivityDraft Pydantic model, retries up to 3 times on validation failure, writes draft to processing_activities table with status=draft. DPOReviewService: transitions record status (draft->under_review->approved/rejected), records review action in audit_log.
backend.failure_handling: Azure OpenAI timeout (>30s): FastAPI BackgroundTask catches exception, sets inference_job status=failed with error_detail; client notified via status polling. Pydantic validation failure after 3 retries: record written with status=needs_clarification and raw_llm_output stored for DPO manual completion. PostgreSQL connection failure: FastAPI startup health check; ACI restart policy on_failure.
backend.backend_risks: LLM hallucination of plausible-sounding but incorrect legal bases (e.g., 'legitimate interests' when 'contractual necessity' is correct) cannot be caught by schema validation; DPO review is the essential human-in-the-loop quality gate for all inferred legal bases

security.auth_design: Azure AD OIDC via Azure Managed Identity; FastAPI dependency injects current_user from Azure AD token; Streamlit front-end uses msal-browser for PKCE flow; DPO role assignment in Azure AD group
security.secrets_management: Azure OpenAI endpoint called via Azure Managed Identity credential (DefaultAzureCredential) — no API key stored in application secrets; PostgreSQL connection string in Azure Key Vault referenced via ACI environment variable injection
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_log table records every DPO action: user_id, action ENUM (draft_created/record_approved/record_amended/record_rejected/export_generated), record_id, timestamp, ip_address, before_state JSON, after_state JSON. FastAPI access logs capture user_id and endpoint for all requests. Azure Monitor alert on any export_generated action for compliance monitoring.
security.security_risks: System inventory descriptions containing PII (employee names, NI numbers in description text) could be logged in LLM request logs; mitigation: PII scrubbing step using presidio before system description is sent to Azure OpenAI

data.entities: processing_activities (id UUID, system_id FK, activity_name TEXT, controller_name TEXT, dpo_contact TEXT, purposes TEXT[], legal_bases processing_legal_basis[], data_subject_categories TEXT[], personal_data_categories TEXT[], recipients TEXT[], third_country_transfers TEXT[], retention_period TEXT, security_measures TEXT, status ENUM draft/under_review/approved/rejected/needs_clarification, inferred_at TIMESTAMP, approved_by UUID, approved_at TIMESTAMP, version INT). data_categories (id UUID, category_name TEXT, sensitivity ENUM ordinary/special_category, gdpr_article_reference TEXT). system_inventory (id UUID, system_name TEXT, description TEXT, data_owner TEXT, business_unit TEXT, api_docs_url TEXT, uploaded_at TIMESTAMP). audit_log (id UUID, user_id UUID, action TEXT, record_id UUID, before_state JSONB, after_state JSONB, timestamp TIMESTAMP, ip_address TEXT).
data.retention_and_deletion: system_inventory records soft-deleted with deleted_at after prototype ends + 90 days; processing_activities retained 3 years from approval_date; audit_log retained 2 years; all tables have row-level soft delete to support GDPR erasure requests for system_inventory descriptions that may contain personal data
data.data_risks: LLM inference outputs stored as draft records may contain hallucinated data category labels; DPO approval step is the mandatory quality gate; no approved record can be exported without explicit DPO approval status

devops.deployment_topology: Single Azure Container Instance running FastAPI + Streamlit in one container image (docker-compose style with supervisord); Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server in same Azure region; Azure OpenAI endpoint in same region (Sweden Central) for data residency
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on PR: pytest with fixtures for mock Azure OpenAI responses, mypy strict type checking, ruff linting; on main merge: docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, ACI restart with new image via az container restart
devops.environments: dev (local Docker with mock Azure OpenAI via LangChain MockLLM), production (ACI in Sweden Central)
devops.devops_risks: ACI does not support zero-downtime restarts; container restart causes 30-60s downtime; acceptable for prototype; production would require AKS with rolling updates

ISS-001.title: No PII scrubbing before system inventory descriptions sent to Azure OpenAI
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan did not include a PII scrubbing step; system inventory descriptions containing employee names, NI numbers, or email addresses in free-text fields would be transmitted to Azure OpenAI as part of the inference prompt, creating a data minimisation violation under GDPR Article 5(1)(c)

history.note: Identified in security review; presidio or equivalent scrubbing required before prompt construction

ISS-002.title: No prompt versioning strategy; prompt changes cannot be regression tested
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described LangChain prompt orchestration but did not specify how prompt templates are versioned; prompt changes that improve accuracy for some system types may degrade others, and there is no mechanism to detect regressions

history.note: Identified in architect review; prompt templates must be versioned with regression fixture tests

latest_recommendations: Add presidio-analyzer PII scrubbing step in SystemInventoryParser before description is stored or used in LLM prompt
Version prompt templates as YAML files in prompts/ directory with semantic version tags; pytest fixtures test inference output against reference JSON for each template version
Add low-confidence flag to RoPA inference output for fields where LLM confidence score is below threshold; route flagged records to DPO clarification queue rather than draft queue

convergence_state.reason: High-severity PII scrubbing gap and medium-severity prompt versioning gap unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Strong overall architecture for an LLM-assisted RoPA prototype with appropriate DPO review workflow, but a high-severity GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation gap exists in the LLM prompt pipeline and a medium-severity prompt versioning gap prevents regression testing of LLM output quality.
strengths: Azure Managed Identity for OpenAI access eliminates API key rotation risk
DPO approval gating before any record export is the correct human-in-the-loop control for high-risk LLM inference
Pydantic structured output validation with LangChain retry is appropriate for reliable GDPR field schema compliance
concerns: Free-text system inventory descriptions may contain PII that would be transmitted to Azure OpenAI without minimisation
Prompt templates stored inline in Python code cannot be versioned or regression tested independently of application code
Low-confidence LLM inferences not distinguished from high-confidence ones in DPO review queue
blocking_issues: PII scrubbing required before system inventory descriptions enter LLM prompt pipeline under GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation principle
recommendations: Integrate presidio-analyzer in SystemInventoryParser.scrub_description() before description is stored to system_inventory table or used in prompt
Move prompt templates to prompts/ropa_inference_v{n}.yaml with version field; pytest parametrize over fixture system descriptions and assert required Article 30 fields present in output
Add confidence_score FLOAT column to processing_activities; LangChain chain returns self-assessed confidence; records below 0.7 threshold get status=needs_clarification automatically

previous_plan.title: GDPR Article 30 RoPA Automation Prototype Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: FastAPI + Streamlit prototype using Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with LangChain structured output to infer GDPR Article 30 RoPA records from system inventory descriptions. DPO review and approval workflow with JSON-LD and PDF export. PostgreSQL for record persistence.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: FastAPI async API with Azure OpenAI GPT-4o structured JSON output via LangChain; Pydantic validation with 3-attempt retry; DPO review via Streamlit; PostgreSQL for RoPA records and audit_log. No PII scrubbing step before LLM prompt construction. Prompt templates stored inline in Python.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC authentication; Azure Managed Identity for OpenAI; Azure Key Vault for DB connection string; HTTPS TLS 1.3; audit_log table. PII scrubbing before LLM prompt not specified. Prompt versioning not addressed.

target_output.thinking_summary: This revision addresses two issues from round 1: ISS-001 requires adding a presidio-analyzer PII scrubbing step in SystemInventoryParser before any system description is stored or used in LLM prompt construction — this is a GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation obligation, not optional. ISS-002 requires moving prompt templates from inline Python code to versioned YAML files with regression test fixtures, so prompt changes can be validated against known-good outputs before deployment. Both fixes require changes to the backend, security, and deployment sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Added presidio-analyzer PII scrubbing step to SystemInventoryParser.scrub_description() in workflows and system_components; added scrubbing to data_model description of system_inventory table; documented scrubbing in security_and_compliance as GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation control
fix_report.expected_outcome: System inventory descriptions stored in PostgreSQL and sent to Azure OpenAI are free of personal data entities (PERSON, NRP, EMAIL_ADDRESS, UK_NHS, UK_NINO); GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation satisfied for LLM prompt pipeline

fix_report.action_taken: Added prompt template versioning strategy to deployment_and_operations and development_guidelines; moved prompt description from inline Python to versioned YAML in prompts/ directory; added regression test fixtures to phased_implementation and development_guidelines
fix_report.expected_outcome: Prompt changes tracked with semantic version tags; pytest parametrized over 10 fixture system descriptions asserts required Article 30 fields present in LLM output; prompt regressions caught before ACI deployment

target_output.title: GDPR Article 30 RoPA Automation Prototype Architecture — Revision 2
target_output.executive_summary: Python 3.12 FastAPI backend with Azure OpenAI GPT-4o structured output and LangChain orchestration, deployed on Azure Container Instances, generating draft GDPR Article 30 RoPA records from enterprise system inventory descriptions. Presidio-analyzer PII scrubbing applied to system descriptions before storage and LLM prompt construction to satisfy GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation. Prompt templates versioned as YAML files with pytest regression fixture tests. Streamlit DPO review interface for approve/amend/reject workflow with JSON-LD and PDF export of approved records.
target_output.architecture_overview: FastAPI async API on Azure Container Instances processes system inventory uploads through a PII scrubbing pipeline (presidio-analyzer entity recognition removing PERSON, EMAIL_ADDRESS, UK_NINO, and UK_NHS entities before storage) before persisting to system_inventory table in Azure PostgreSQL. LLM inference jobs are triggered asynchronously via FastAPI BackgroundTasks; the RoPAInferenceService constructs a versioned prompt from prompts/ropa_inference_v{n}.yaml, calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with response_format json_schema, validates output against ProcessingActivityDraft Pydantic model, and retries up to 3 times on validation failure. Records with confidence_score below 0.7 or persistent validation failure are written with status=needs_clarification and routed to a DPO clarification queue. DPOs interact via Streamlit multi-page UI, reviewing drafts, amending fields, and approving records before JSON-LD or PDF export. All DPO actions recorded in audit_log for GDPR Article 30 accountability.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12 with FastAPI (async), LangChain 0.3 with AzureChatOpenAI chain and StructuredOutputParser, presidio-analyzer 2.2 for PII entity detection, Pydantic v2 for RoPA record validation, python-jose for Azure AD JWT validation. LLM: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o deployment in Sweden Central with structured JSON output (response_format json_schema). Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server with Azure Disk Encryption. Frontend: Streamlit 1.35 with msal-browser PKCE flow for Azure AD auth. CI: GitHub Actions with pytest, mypy strict, ruff. Hosting: Azure Container Instances (Sweden Central), Azure Container Registry.
target_output.functional_feature_map: System Inventory Upload: CSV with columns system_name, description, data_owner, business_unit, api_docs_url; presidio scrubs description before storage; per-row validation report shows scrubbed entity types. LLM RoPA Inference: async GPT-4o inference producing ProcessingActivityDraft with all GDPR Article 30 mandatory fields: controller_name, dpo_contact, purposes[], legal_bases[], data_subject_categories[], personal_data_categories[], recipients[], third_country_transfers[], retention_period, security_measures, confidence_score. DPO Review Queue: pending drafts with confidence score badges; low-confidence and needs_clarification records in separate queue. DPO Amendment Flow: inline field editing with field-level Article 30 compliance hints; approve/reject buttons with mandatory comment on rejection. RoPA Registry: approved records filterable by business_unit, legal_basis, data_subject_category. Coverage Gap Analytics: heatmap of business_units vs personal_data_categories with missing-record gaps highlighted. Export: JSON-LD using DPVCG Data Privacy Vocabulary schema; PDF via WeasyPrint from HTML template.
target_output.system_components: Azure Container Instance running FastAPI API server (port 8000) and Streamlit UI server (port 8501) managed by supervisord. Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (General Purpose 4 vCores). Azure Container Registry for container image storage. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o deployment in Sweden Central (same region as ACI for data residency). Azure Key Vault storing DB connection string injected as ACI environment variable via managed identity reference. SystemInventoryParser with presidio-analyzer scrubbing pipeline (PERSON, EMAIL_ADDRESS, UK_NINO, UK_NHS recognisers). RoPAInferenceService with LangChain AzureChatOpenAI chain, versioned YAML prompt loader, ProcessingActivityDraft Pydantic model, and 3-attempt retry. DPOReviewService with audit_log writer. ExportService generating JSON-LD and PDF.
target_output.workflows: System Inventory Upload with PII Scrubbing: DPO uploads CSV via Streamlit. FastAPI POST /api/inventory parses CSV rows. For each row, SystemInventoryParser.scrub_description() runs presidio-analyzer with UK-configured recognisers (PERSON, EMAIL_ADDRESS, UK_NHS, UK_NINO); detected entities replaced with [PERSON_REDACTED], [NHS_NUMBER_REDACTED], etc. Scrubbed description stored to system_inventory table. Scrubbing report returned to UI showing entity types removed per row. LLM RoPA Inference: DPO selects systems for inference and triggers POST /api/infer with system_id[]. FastAPI creates inference_job records and returns job_ids. BackgroundTask for each job: loads prompt template from prompts/ropa_inference_v2.yaml, constructs prompt with scrubbed system description and GDPR Article 30 field taxonomy, calls AzureChatOpenAI with response_format json_schema. LangChain StructuredOutputParser validates against ProcessingActivityDraft Pydantic model; on ValidationError retries up to 3 times with error feedback appended to prompt. On success: confidence_score extracted from LLM output; if score < 0.7 status=needs_clarification, else status=draft. Record written to processing_activities. DPO Review: DPO opens draft_review page, selects pending record. Streamlit displays all Article 30 fields with compliance hint tooltips. DPO amends legal_basis or data_category fields; auto-save PATCH to /api/records/{id}/draft on change. DPO clicks Approve: PATCH /api/records/{id}/approve transitions status=approved, writes audit_log entry with before/after state.
target_output.data_model: system_inventory (id UUID PK, system_name TEXT NOT NULL, description_scrubbed TEXT, description_original_hash TEXT (SHA-256 of original for audit), data_owner TEXT, business_unit TEXT, api_docs_url TEXT, scrubbing_report JSONB (entities_removed[]), uploaded_at TIMESTAMP, uploaded_by UUID, deleted_at TIMESTAMP). processing_activities (id UUID PK, system_id UUID FK, activity_name TEXT NOT NULL, controller_name TEXT, dpo_contact TEXT, purposes TEXT[], legal_bases processing_legal_basis[] (ENUM: consent/contract/legal_obligation/vital_interests/public_task/legitimate_interests), data_subject_categories TEXT[], personal_data_categories TEXT[], recipients TEXT[], third_country_transfers TEXT[], retention_period TEXT, security_measures TEXT, confidence_score FLOAT, status ENUM draft/under_review/approved/rejected/needs_clarification, inferred_at TIMESTAMP, approved_by UUID FK, approved_at TIMESTAMP, version INT DEFAULT 1, prompt_template_version TEXT). audit_log (id UUID PK, user_id UUID NOT NULL, action TEXT NOT NULL, record_id UUID, before_state JSONB, after_state JSONB, timestamp TIMESTAMP NOT NULL, ip_address INET). Prompt versioning: prompts/ropa_inference_v1.yaml, prompts/ropa_inference_v2.yaml stored in repository with semantic version in filename; processing_activities.prompt_template_version records which prompt version produced each draft.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/inventory — multipart CSV upload; returns {systems_imported: INT, scrubbing_report: [{system_id, entities_removed: [{entity_type, count}]}]}. POST /api/infer — body {system_ids: UUID[]}; returns {job_ids: [{system_id, job_id}]}; inference runs asynchronously. GET /api/infer/{job_id}/status — returns {status: pending/completed/failed, record_id UUID, confidence_score FLOAT}. GET /api/records — query params: status, business_unit, legal_basis; returns paginated list of ProcessingActivitySummary. GET /api/records/{id} — full ProcessingActivityDetail with all Article 30 fields. PATCH /api/records/{id}/draft — partial update for in-progress amendments. PATCH /api/records/{id}/approve — DPO role required; sets status=approved, records audit entry. POST /api/export — body {record_ids: UUID[], format: json_ld|pdf}; returns export file or presigned Azure Blob URL.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure AD OIDC authentication enforced on all FastAPI endpoints via Depends(get_current_user); Streamlit uses msal-browser PKCE flow with DPO role validation before any page renders. GDPR Article 5(1)(c) data minimisation: presidio-analyzer scrubs system descriptions before storage and LLM prompt construction, removing PERSON, EMAIL_ADDRESS, UK_NHS, and UK_NINO entities; scrubbing report stored in system_inventory.scrubbing_report JSONB for audit evidence. Azure OpenAI called via DefaultAzureCredential (Managed Identity) — no API key stored in application secrets; Azure Key Vault stores PostgreSQL connection string accessed via ACI managed identity. Audit log records all DPO actions (draft review, approve, reject, export) with before_state/after_state JSONB for GDPR Article 30 accountability demonstration. Processing_activities.prompt_template_version column enables post-hoc audit of which prompt version produced a given RoPA draft. GDPR Article 35 DPIA trigger: processing_activities with legal_basis=legitimate_interests and special_category personal data trigger a DPIA_recommended flag visible in the DPO registry view.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI on every PR: pytest (including mock Azure OpenAI fixtures via pytest-vcr cassettes, parametrized over 10 system inventory fixture files asserting required Article 30 fields present), mypy strict type checking, ruff linting; merge to main: docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, az container restart --name ropa-prototype --resource-group privacy-rg to pull new image. Prompt template versioning: prompt files in prompts/ropa_inference_v{n}.yaml committed to repository; version bumped in PR when prompt changes; CI runs regression tests against all prior fixture cassettes to detect output regressions; prompt_template_version field in processing_activities records which version produced each draft. Azure Container Instance restart policy: on_failure with max 3 restarts before alert. Azure Monitor alerts: inference_job failure rate > 20% over 30 minutes; ACI container restarts > 2 per hour.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor application logs from FastAPI structured JSON logging (user_id, endpoint, status_code, duration_ms, llm_tokens_used on inference endpoints). Custom metrics tracked in Application Insights: ropa_inference_duration_seconds (p50/p95 by prompt_template_version), ropa_confidence_score_distribution (histogram to detect prompt quality drift), ropa_needs_clarification_rate (pct of inferences below 0.7 confidence — leading indicator of prompt regression), dpo_approval_rate (approved vs rejected per week). Coverage gap analytics: Streamlit gap_analytics page queries processing_activities and system_inventory to compute business_unit x data_subject_category coverage matrix; cells with zero approved records shown in amber; cells with needs_clarification records shown in yellow.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Azure Container Instances: ~$80/month (2 vCPU, 4GB). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server 4 vCores: ~$150/month. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o tokens: ~$0.50 per 100-system inference run at ~500 tokens per system description; $50/month for daily prototype runs. Azure Container Registry: ~$5/month. Total ~$285/month, well within $500/month prototype budget. Azure OpenAI token costs scale linearly with number of system descriptions per run; 1000-system production run costs ~$5 per run.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): PostgreSQL schema with processing_activities, system_inventory, audit_log; SystemInventoryParser with presidio scrubbing; RoPAInferenceService with LangChain GPT-4o chain and Pydantic validation; FastAPI endpoints for upload, infer, review, approve, export; Streamlit DPO review UI with draft_review and ropa_registry pages; prompts/ropa_inference_v1.yaml with 5 regression fixture tests; GitHub Actions CI; ACI deployment. DPO user testing cycle 1: 10 real system descriptions, measure field accuracy against DPO manual baseline. Phase 2 Prompt Tuning (3 weeks): Analyse cycle 1 feedback to identify consistently wrong legal_basis and data_category inferences; update prompts/ropa_inference_v2.yaml with corrected examples; add confidence_score threshold routing to needs_clarification queue; expand regression fixtures to 20 systems. DPO user testing cycle 2: re-test with v2 prompt; target >80% field-level accuracy. Phase 3 Coverage and Export (2 weeks): gap_analytics page, DPIA trigger flag for high-risk processing, JSON-LD export using DPVCG vocabulary, PDF export via WeasyPrint.
target_output.development_guidelines: Pydantic v2 strict mode for all API request/response models; no untyped dicts in FastAPI handlers. Prompt templates are YAML files, never inline Python f-strings — any prompt change requires a new version file and regression test PR. pytest fixtures for LLM inference use pytest-vcr cassettes to replay Azure OpenAI responses deterministically; cassettes committed to tests/cassettes/ and regenerated only when deliberately updating test expectations. presidio scrubbing applied before any system description is written to database or passed to LLM — verified by integration test asserting no PERSON entities in system_inventory.description_scrubbed column across all fixture uploads. mypy strict enforced in CI with no type: ignore annotations in production code.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: LLM hallucination of incorrect legal basis (e.g., 'consent' when 'legal obligation' is correct) cannot be caught by Pydantic schema validation since any valid ENUM value passes; DPO review is the essential quality gate and cannot be made optional. Mitigation: confidence_score < 0.7 automatically routes to needs_clarification, reducing DPO review burden for high-confidence inferences. Risk: Presidio scrubbing false positives redact non-PII tokens (e.g., product names containing common surnames); mitigation: scrubbing_report shown to DPO for manual review of redacted entities before inference is triggered. Risk: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o model version updates change inference output without notice; mitigation: prompt regression tests in CI detect output changes before deployment. Tradeoff: Azure Container Instances vs AKS — ACI chosen for prototype simplicity at the cost of 30-60s restart downtime; production deployment would use AKS with rolling updates. Tradeoff: Streamlit over React for DPO UI — Streamlit enables rapid iteration and avoids frontend build overhead for a prototype; scalability and advanced UX features require a custom frontend in production.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose presidio-analyzer over manual regex for PII scrubbing — presidio provides trained NER models for UK-specific entities (UK_NHS, UK_NINO) that regex cannot reliably detect. Chose YAML-based prompt versioning over LangSmith LLM ops platform — simpler dependency footprint appropriate for prototype; LangSmith considered for production. Chose confidence_score threshold at 0.7 based on initial prompt testing showing GPT-4o self-reported confidence correlates with DPO correction rate; threshold subject to revision after cycle 1 user testing data. Chose JSON-LD export using DPVCG Data Privacy Vocabulary for interoperability with OneTrust and TrustArc import formats that DPOs are familiar with.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision for GDPR RoPA automation prototype; added presidio PII scrubbing before LLM prompt (ISS-001) and prompt YAML versioning with regression tests (ISS-002).

profile.domain: Financial Crime Real-Time Sanctions Screening Research

project_goal.value: Research prototype demonstrating real-time OFAC SDN list sanctions screening of payment instructions using a streaming pipeline that replays historical payment batches against the current watchlist with sub-second match latency for financial crime research teams
project_goal.rationale: Validates real-time streaming screening architecture before production implementation decision

target_users.value: Financial crime researchers and AML engineers at financial institutions evaluating streaming sanctions screening architectures
target_users.rationale: Internal research consumers testing feasibility of sub-second OFAC screening against live SDN updates

project_class.rationale: Exploratory prototype demonstrating screening architecture; not production payment processing

capabilities.rationale: Streaming pipeline with real-time matching, persistent match result storage, and Grafana analytics; no user-facing frontend beyond Grafana dashboards

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time streaming with hot-reload watchlist updates and fuzzy entity matching requires advanced distributed systems design

risk_level.rationale: Processes financial transaction data containing party names, amounts, and account identifiers; OFAC compliance is a regulatory requirement

data_sensitivity.rationale: Payment instructions contain account numbers, amounts, counterparty names, and bank routing information

external_exposure.rationale: Research prototype accessible only to internal financial crime research team on GCP VPC

access_model.value: Google Workspace SSO for Grafana dashboards and prototype admin interface; GCP IAM service accounts for pipeline components
access_model.rationale: Google Workspace SSO for researcher access; IAM for pipeline component authorization

feature_scope.value: Payment instruction streaming from Kafka, fuzzy entity name matching against in-memory OFAC SDN watchlist, match result persistence in ClickHouse, hot-reload of SDN delta updates without pipeline restart, Grafana dashboard for match rate and latency metrics, historical batch replay mode for research validation
feature_scope.rationale: Complete prototype feature set for architecture validation

mvp_scope.value: Kafka consumer with in-memory watchlist and basic Jaro-Winkler name matching emitting results to ClickHouse; Grafana dashboard showing match rate and p99 latency
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimal viable demonstration of streaming screening concept

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 for Kafka and ClickHouse connections; GCP IAM for pipeline component authentication; payment instruction data encrypted at rest in ClickHouse using AES-256; no raw payment data in Grafana panels
security_baseline.rationale: Minimum controls for financial data in research environment

frontend_stack.value: Grafana 10 dashboards for research team analytics and match monitoring; no custom web frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: Grafana sufficient for research visualization needs without custom UI investment

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Apache Kafka Streams consumer using confluent-kafka-python; in-memory watchlist index with Jaro-Winkler fuzzy matching via jellyfish library
backend_stack.rationale: Python for rapid prototype iteration; confluent-kafka-python for production-grade Kafka integration; jellyfish for battle-tested name similarity algorithms

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for match results and payment screening records; Redis 7 for watchlist snapshot versioning and pipeline coordination
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse for high-throughput append-only match result storage with analytical query performance; Redis for hot-reload coordination across consumer replicas

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot for Python consumer pods and coordination services; GCP Managed Kafka (or Confluent Cloud on GCP) for Kafka cluster; ClickHouse Cloud or self-managed ClickHouse on GCE
hosting_target.rationale: GKE Autopilot for managed Kubernetes without node management; Managed Kafka reduces operational overhead in research context

privacy_retention_policy.value: Payment instruction data retained 90 days in ClickHouse for research replay; match results retained 1 year for research documentation; no production customer data used - synthetic or anonymized historical data only
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Research data retention policy; no real customer PII in prototype

future_scope.value: Production-grade implementation with formal OFAC hit review workflow, SAR filing integration, and real-time payment processing integration if prototype validates architecture
future_scope.rationale: Prototype success gates production implementation decision

constraints.value: Sub-second p99 match latency for payment instructions; watchlist hot-reload must not interrupt active screening pipeline; prototype must replay 10M historical payment instructions in under 2 hours for research validation
constraints.rationale: Latency and replay throughput are the core research validation metrics

observability_baseline.value: Grafana dashboards backed by Prometheus metrics from Python consumer pods; ClickHouse query metrics; custom metrics: match_rate, false_positive_rate, watchlist_reload_latency_ms, kafka_consumer_lag_ms
observability_baseline.rationale: Research team needs real-time visibility into screening quality and pipeline performance metrics

execution_preference.value: Iterative prototype sprints: streaming baseline first, then fuzzy matching quality improvements, then hot-reload watchlist updates, then batch replay performance tuning
execution_preference.rationale: Iterative approach allows research team to validate each capability independently

compliance_context.value: OFAC SDN compliance requirements for financial sanctions screening; FinCEN Guidance FIN-2020-A002 on real-time payments screening expectations; prototype must document false negative rate for regulatory validation purposes
compliance_context.rationale: OFAC and FinCEN requirements govern production screening implementation decisions based on prototype findings

project.goal: Sub-second OFAC SDN screening prototype for payment instruction stream research validation
project.target_users: Financial crime research team on GCP internal network
project.timeline: Round 2 addressing hot-reload blocking and matching threshold calibration

security.auth: GKE Workload Identity + Kafka mTLS
security.encryption: ClickHouse AES-256 column encryption for party names

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: pytest, Cloud Build Docker

constraints.reload: Watchlist hot-reload must not interrupt active screening

reasoner_reviews.product: ISS-001 is fundamental to the prototype's research validity: if OFAC SDN updates (which happen multiple times per day) cause 400ms screening gaps, the prototype cannot claim sub-second continuity. ISS-002 directly affects the false negative rate measurement which is the core metric for regulatory evaluation. Both must be resolved before the research findings are presented to the AML compliance team.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Double-buffered watchlist index is the standard pattern for hot-reload without blocking: maintain active_index and standby_index objects; reload OFAC delta into standby while active_index serves all screening; atomic swap via Python threading.Event and index reference reassignment; consumers that started a match against old active_index complete naturally. For ISS-002, log the similarity_score and script_type for every candidate match to ClickHouse to enable post-hoc threshold sensitivity analysis across the historical replay dataset.
reasoner_reviews.security: The DoubleBufferedWatchlistIndex atomic swap must be thread-safe using Python threading.Lock on the pointer swap only, not on the rebuild. The standby index rebuild is single-threaded and does not hold any lock. All consumer threads read from the active_index reference which is an atomic Python object reference assignment (GIL-protected).
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 10M instruction batch replay constraint requires the consumer group to process at least 1,400 instructions/second across all pods. At 3 consumer pods, each pod must process ~500 instructions/second. DoubleBufferedWatchlistIndex rebuild should add negligible latency to active screening during the swap window.

backend.service_design: Python 3.12 consumer application: KafkaConsumer (confluent-kafka-python, consumer group screening_workers), DoubleBufferedWatchlistIndex (active/standby atomically swapped on reload), MultiTierMatcher (exact canonical -> script-type-aware Jaro-Winkler), ScreeningResultEmitter (Kafka producer to screening_results topic), ClickHouseWriter (batch insert to match_results table)
backend.api_patterns: Kafka consumer group with auto-offset-commit disabled; manual offset commit after ClickHouse write confirmed; at-least-once delivery with idempotent result deduplication via payment_instruction_id
backend.failure_handling: Consumer crash triggers Kubernetes pod restart with last committed offset replay; ClickHouse writer retry with exponential backoff on connection failure; watchlist reload failure logs error and continues with current watchlist version
backend.scaling_notes: GKE HorizontalPodAutoscaler on consumer group lag metric via Kafka consumer lag Prometheus exporter; each consumer pod holds its own DoubleBufferedWatchlistIndex replica coordinated via Redis version counter

security.auth_design: GKE Workload Identity for GCP service account binding to consumer pods; Kafka mTLS for broker connections; ClickHouse user per service with read-only for Grafana and write-only for consumer
security.privacy_controls: Payment instruction party names stored in ClickHouse with AES-256 column encryption; Grafana panels display anonymized entity_id not raw party names; no raw payment data in Prometheus metrics labels
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All watchlist reload events logged to ClickHouse watchlist_events table with reload timestamp, delta_version, entity_count_change, reload_duration_ms; screening_decisions logged with payment_instruction_id, match_score, threshold_used, decision

data.entities: payment_instructions (id, originator_name, beneficiary_name, amount, currency, originator_account, beneficiary_account, batch_id, instruction_timestamp), watchlist_snapshots (id, version, source ENUM ofac_sdn, entity_count, loaded_at), match_candidates (id, payment_id, watchlist_entity_id, similarity_score, script_type, threshold_used, match_result ENUM hit/no_hit/review, screened_at), screening_decisions (id, payment_id, final_decision, max_score, processing_ms, watchlist_version), entity_scores (id, entity_name, canonical_forms[], script_type, source_program)
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree tables for payment_instructions and match_candidates with ReplicatedMergeTree for HA; Redis HASH for watchlist version coordination across consumer pods; in-memory DoubleBufferedWatchlistIndex in each consumer pod
data.analytics_events: Grafana queries: SELECT quantile(0.99)(processing_ms) FROM screening_decisions WHERE screened_at > now() - interval 5 minute; SELECT countIf(final_decision='hit')/count() AS match_rate FROM screening_decisions GROUP BY toStartOfMinute(screened_at)

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot with Kubernetes Deployment for consumer pods (3 replicas); Kafka on Confluent Cloud GCP region; ClickHouse on GCE 8vCPU instance with local SSD; Redis on GCP Memorystore; Grafana on GKE
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for matcher and consumer logic, Cloud Build for Docker image; GKE rolling update via kubectl set image on main merge
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics from consumer pods: kafka_consumer_lag, screening_throughput_per_second, watchlist_reload_duration_ms, match_rate_by_script_type. Grafana dashboards: Screening Performance, Watchlist Health, False Positive/Negative Analysis

ISS-001.title: Watchlist hot-reload blocks active consumer goroutines during index rebuild
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 watchlist reload implementation acquires a global write lock on the in-memory matching index during OFAC delta file ingestion, causing all active screening goroutines to block for the full rebuild duration which averages 400ms for a full SDN reload

history.note: Global write lock on matching index during hot-reload interrupts screening pipeline

ISS-002.title: Jaro-Winkler matching threshold not calibrated against SDN name variants
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 uses a fixed Jaro-Winkler similarity threshold of 0.85 without any calibration against known SDN name variants and transliterations; internal testing against OFAC name variant dataset shows 12% false negative rate at 0.85 threshold for Arabic and Cyrillic name transliterations

history.note: Fixed 0.85 threshold produces 12% false negative rate on SDN name variant test set

latest_recommendations: Replace global write lock with double-buffered watchlist index: maintain active and standby WatchlistIndex instances; rebuild standby while active serves screening requests; atomic pointer swap on rebuild completion

Implement a multi-tier matching strategy: exact canonical match first, then Jaro-Winkler with dynamic threshold by script type (0.90 for Latin, 0.87 for Cyrillic transliterations, 0.85 for Arabic transliterations); calibrate against OFAC publically documented name variant test cases
